Standard Operating Procedures

🦷 Orthodontic Clinical Manual

Last updated: March 2026  ·  For internal clinical use only
🤝
The Four Agreements
Our guiding principles — how we show up for our patients and each other
▾
I
Be Impeccable with Your Word
Speak with integrity. Say only what you mean. Use the power of your word in the direction of truth and love — with patients, colleagues, and yourself.
II
Don't Take Anything Personally
Nothing others do is because of you. When you are immune to the opinions and actions of others, you won't be the victim of needless suffering.
III
Don't Make Assumptions
Find the courage to ask questions and express what you really want. Communicate clearly to avoid misunderstandings, sadness, and drama.
IV
Always Do Your Best
Your best will change from moment to moment. Simply do your best under any circumstance, and you will avoid self-judgment, self-abuse, and regret.
🧑‍⚕️ New Patient 2 procedures ▾
📂
New Patient Records Workflow
SOP-NP-004  ·  Clinical Assistant & Treatment Coordinator
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-NP-004
Appt Code
407
Staff
CA + TC

Before Patient Arrives

  • Confirm intake forms are complete in Edge and patient chart is open.
  • Queue panoramic X-ray unit — confirm it's powered on and bite block is sanitized.
  • Open patient record on the scanner (iTero or Allied Star); confirm name and DOB match.
  • Confirm Olympus OMD camera settings per SOP-RC-003 (Mode A · ISO 200 · Flash Fill · Spot metering).
  • Prepare cheek retractors, intraoral mirror, and photography station.

Records — Sequence (Always in This Order)

  1. Panoramic X-ray — take before patient is seated chairside. Review image immediately; retake if unclear.
  2. Cephalometric X-ray — if indicated.
  3. Extraoral photos (3) — full face frontal, full face smiling, lateral profile. Camera at F8.
  4. Intraoral photos (7) — frontal retracted, bilateral buccal, upper occlusal, lower occlusal, and any supplemental views. Camera at F22.
  5. Intraoral scan (iTero or Allied Star) — upper arch → lower arch → buccal bite. Confirm no voids before patient leaves.
⚠️ All records must be uploaded and the pano printed before Dr. Akhavan arrives chairside. Do not seat the patient for the exam until records are complete and flagged in Edge.

After Records — Upload & Print

  1. Upload all photos to the patient chart immediately — do not batch for later.
  2. Print the panoramic X-ray and place it where Dr. Akhavan can review it chairside.
  3. Confirm scan is saved and synced to the cloud.
  4. Mark chart as "Records Complete" in Edge.

TC Handoff (After Exam)

  • TC greets patient and family in the consultation room after Dr. Akhavan completes the exam.
  • Confirm treatment plan has been entered in Edge by Dr. Akhavan.
  • Proceed to financial presentation — see SOP-FO-005.

Example: Complete Orthodontic Records

The photo set below shows a properly completed records appointment — full extraoral and intraoral series with corresponding radiographs.

Patient name removed for privacy. Final records.

Example: Orthodontic Radiographs

Both a lateral cephalometric and panoramic radiograph are required at the records appointment.

Lateral Cephalometric

Panoramic (OPG)

📊
Progress Records Workflow
SOP-NP-005  ·  Clinical Assistant
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-NP-005
When
Mid-treatment (Pan & Repo checkpoint) or as directed by Dr. A
Staff
Clinical Assistant

Before Patient Arrives

  • Confirm patient chart is open in Edge and the appointment is flagged for progress records.
  • Queue panoramic X-ray unit — confirm it's powered on and bite block is sanitized.
  • Open patient record on the scanner (iTero or Allied Star); confirm name and DOB match.
  • Confirm Olympus OMD camera settings per SOP-RC-003 (Mode A · ISO 200 · Flash Fill · Spot metering).

Records — Sequence

  1. Panoramic X-ray — evaluate root parallelism and overall bone levels. Review immediately; retake if unclear. Print for Dr. A to review chairside.
  2. Extraoral photos (3) — full face frontal, full face smiling, lateral profile. Camera at F8.
  3. Intraoral photos (7) — frontal retracted, bilateral buccal, upper occlusal, lower occlusal, and any supplemental views. Camera at F22.
  4. Intraoral scan (iTero or Allied Star) — upper arch → lower arch → buccal bite. Confirm no voids before patient leaves.
⚠️ All records must be uploaded and the pano printed before Dr. Akhavan arrives chairside. Do not proceed with repo or wire changes until Dr. A has reviewed the panoramic X-ray. All records must be fully uploaded before the next patient is brought back.

After Records — Upload & Flag

  1. Upload all photos to the patient chart immediately.
  2. Print the panoramic X-ray and place it where Dr. Akhavan can review it chairside.
  3. Confirm scan is saved and synced.
  4. Mark chart as "Progress Records Complete" in Edge.
🔧 Clinical Procedures 28 procedures ▾
🪑
Seating a Patient
SOP-CL-021  ·  Clinical Assistant
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-021
Staff
Clinical Assistant
Applies To
Every appointment, every patient

Greeting & Escort

  1. Call the patient from the waiting room using their first name.
  2. Greet them warmly and make eye contact. Include parents or guardians — invite them to come chairside with the patient.
  3. Escort the patient to the assigned chair and help them get comfortable.

Introduce the Appointment

  1. Before beginning any clinical work, give the patient a brief overview of what will happen today — walk them through the appointment from start to finish.
  2. Ask if they have any questions before you get started.

Apply PPE

  1. Place the protective eyewear on the patient before any clinical work begins.
  2. Ensure your own PPE is in place: gloves, mask, and eyewear.
💡 A warm, clear introduction at the start of every appointment reduces patient anxiety and sets a professional tone. Parents who are kept informed chairside tend to have higher treatment compliance.
💬
Chairside Scripting & Pre-Doctor Checklist
SOP-CL-022  ·  Clinical Assistant
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-022
Staff
Clinical Assistant
Applies To
All appointment types

Pre-Doctor Checklist

Before calling the doctor to the chair, confirm the following:

  • Treatment card is pulled up and current in Edge.
  • ClinCheck is open and visible (aligner patients).
  • Panoramic X-ray is visible (braces and records patients).
  • Relevant notes from last visit have been reviewed.

Scripting by Patient Type

🦷 Braces Patients
  • How have you been feeling since your last appointment?
  • Any discomfort or soreness after your last wire change?
  • Are you wearing your elastics as directed? Any issues?
  • Any brackets that feel loose or wires that are poking?
📦 Aligner Patients
  • What tray number are you on right now?
  • Are you wearing your aligners for the full recommended hours per day?
  • Any discomfort, tight spots, or attachments that feel rough?
  • Have you had any trays that didn't seem to track or fit correctly?
🔒 Retention Patients
  • How are your retainers fitting — any tightness or looseness?
  • Are you wearing them as directed (full-time or nights)?
  • Have you noticed any tooth movement or shifting?
🔧 Appliance Patients
  • Is the appliance comfortable? Any sore spots?
  • Are you (or parents) activating it as directed?
  • Any questions about what the appliance is doing or how it works?
✅
Dismissing a Patient
SOP-CL-023  ·  Clinical Assistant
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-023
Staff
Clinical Assistant
Applies To
Every appointment, every patient

Before the Patient Leaves the Chair

  1. Complete chart notes in Edge before the patient gets up — document what was done, what was observed, and any patient-reported concerns.
  2. Remove protective eyewear from the patient.
  3. Let the patient and parent know what their next appointment type is and approximately how long it will take. (e.g., "Your next visit is an adjustment — that's about 20 minutes.")
  4. Answer any questions and thank the patient before they leave the bay.

Chair Turnover

  1. Strip all barriers: headrest cover, light handle covers, air/water syringe covers, mouse barrier.
  2. Dispose of single-use items; transfer contaminated instruments to the sterilization area.
  3. Wipe surfaces per SOP-IC-003.
  4. Re-dress the chair with fresh barriers and restock disposable supplies (suction tips, gloves, etc.).
  5. Set up for the next patient before leaving the bay — pull up their chart, queue any needed instruments or trays.
💡 Completing chart notes chairside (before the patient stands up) keeps documentation accurate and prevents end-of-day charting backlogs.
📢
Patient Instructions by Appointment Type
SOP-CL-010  ·  Clinical Assistant
Communication
▾
📋 Deliver these instructions at every applicable appointment. Consistency across all team members is essential — give the same key points each time.

🦷 Braces Bonding

  • Brush at least 2×/day for 2 minutes (ideally after every meal).
  • Floss once a day.
  • Avoid: crunchy (popcorn, chips, ice), sticky (caramel, gum, licorice), hard (nuts, hard candies), and foods requiring biting into (corn on the cob, apples, carrots, ribs, wings).
  • Give the patient the braces survival kit; show how to use wax and floss with braces.
  • Soreness is normal the first week — encourage ibuprofen or cold treats (frozen yogurt, ice cream).
  • Front bracket breaks: call the office. Back bracket break: can wait until the next appointment.

📦 Aligner Delivery (NLA / Invisalign)

  • Wear aligners at least 22 hours per day.
  • Invisalign: change trays every 7 days. In-house (NLA): every 10 days.
  • Remove only to eat and brush. Clear liquids are OK with aligners in.
  • Always keep the last tray as a backup — never throw it away.
  • If a tray is lost or broken: move to the next one. If it's too tight, call the office.
  • Soak trays in denture cleaner at least once a day. Never wrap in a paper towel or napkin.
  • A lisp for the first 1–2 days is normal; it resolves quickly.
  • If an attachment comes off, teeth continue to track — it can wait until the next visit.

🪀 Elastic Wear

  • Wear elastics 24/7 — remove only to eat and brush.
  • Show patient how to hook up elastics on one side, then let them practice the other side.
  • Draw the elastic pattern on the patient's report card and explain the reason (e.g., correcting overbite, closing the bite).
  • Give patient 2 bags of elastics. Confirm proper placement at every visit.

🔧 RPE (Palatal Expander)

  • Show parents how to turn the RPE; let them practice one turn before leaving.
  • Recommend turning at nighttime before bed with the patient lying down for better visibility.
  • Normal to salivate more or sneeze when turning — reassure patient and parent.
  • Takes 24–48 hours to fully adjust to the appliance.
  • Avoid sticky and hard foods while wearing the expander.
  • Provide a calendar with the number of turns and activation schedule.
  • If hooks on the 6's are irritating: give wax to place on hooks until patient adjusts.

🔩 Herbst Appliance

  • Soreness in the jaw joints and muscles is very normal the first 1–2 weeks — this is the jaw adapting to its new position. Ibuprofen or acetaminophen can help.
  • Avoid: hard, crunchy, and sticky foods (same restrictions as braces). Do not bite into anything with your front teeth — cut food into small pieces.
  • Brush thoroughly around all the Herbst components, bands, and tubes. Use a proxy brush or interproximal brush to clean under the metal arms.
  • The appliance is fixed and cannot be removed — it works 24/7. This is expected and intentional.
  • You may notice a small gap opening between your upper and lower front teeth — this is part of how the appliance works and will close over time.
  • Call the office if: a band becomes loose or the arm disconnects from its tube. Do not try to reattach it yourself.
  • Speech may feel different for the first few days — reading aloud helps it normalize faster.

🔩 MARA Appliance

  • Soreness in the jaw muscles is common the first 1–2 weeks as your bite adjusts. OTC pain relievers (ibuprofen/acetaminophen) can help.
  • Avoid hard, sticky, and chewy foods — the same guidelines as braces apply. Chewing gum is not allowed.
  • Brush thoroughly around the bands and all metal components with a soft toothbrush. Use a proxy brush to clean between the appliance and your gum tissue.
  • The MARA is a fixed appliance — it stays in at all times and works continuously to move your jaw forward.
  • You may feel a temporary difficulty fully closing your back teeth together — this resolves as the jaw adapts.
  • Call the office if any band comes loose or you notice a component is not seating correctly.

📿 Removable Lower Schwartz Expander

  • Wear the expander full time — remove only to eat and brush your teeth. The more you wear it, the faster your treatment goes.
  • To insert: seat the appliance with your fingers, pressing firmly but gently until it snaps into place. Never bite it into position — this can crack the acrylic.
  • To remove: use your fingernail or thumbnail to gently unhook it from the back teeth on both sides at the same time.
  • Turn the expansion screw 1 turn per week. Use the key provided — one full quarter turn at a time. Write it on the calendar given to you.
  • Soreness for 24–48 hours after each turn is normal — especially in the teeth and lower jaw.
  • Clean the appliance with a soft toothbrush and liquid soap or denture cleaner. Do not use toothpaste — it scratches acrylic. Rinse thoroughly before reinserting.
  • Keep the expander in its case whenever it is not in your mouth — never wrap it in a napkin or leave it exposed (pets love to chew them).
  • Avoid hot liquids while wearing the appliance — heat can warp the acrylic.
  • Call the office if the appliance cracks, no longer fits snugly, or if you lose it.

💎 Retainer Delivery

  • First 5 days: wear retainers full time (remove only to eat and brush).
  • After day 5: nighttime only — for life (or as long as they want their teeth straight).
  • Keep away from heat (avoid leaving in a hot car) and away from pets.
  • Do not use toothpaste or soak in Listerine to clean.
  • Replacement: $125/retainer with 3D model or $250 without 3D model.
  • Document in the patient chart: retainer type delivered (In-House Essix / Hawley / DB), upper/lower, and whether 3D model was given.
Bonding (Braces)
SOP-CL-001  ·  Clinical Assistant
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-001
Dr. Time
10 min max
Edge Code(s)
202–221 (Bonding Braces)

Tray Setup

NLO standard bonding tray setup

  • Set up the tray fully before seating the patient.
  • Print the panoramic X-ray and have it ready for Dr. A.
  • Complete and organize the bracket pad.

Patient Presentation

  • Activate lightbar to Orange.
  • Review the treatment plan before Dr. A arrives (extraction request, crowns, OCS vs. CCS, limited vs. comprehensive vs. Phase I).
  • Print the panoramic X-ray and have it ready before Dr. A arrives chairside.

Isolation

  • Use proper isolation technique (cheek retractors, cotton rolls, dryangles, etc.).
  • Manage excess saliva throughout bonding using suction.

Bonding

  • Follow the correct bonding sequence ↓
  • Place each bracket with correct orientation and height.
  • Place occlusal bite blocks per the protocol below — required at every initial bonding appointment for braces patients.

Occlusal Bite Block (Turbo) Placement Protocol

Bite blocks are placed at the initial bonding appointment for all braces patients to prevent bracket fracture during occlusion. Follow the placement decision tree in order:

PriorityLocationCondition
1st choice Occlusal of lower 6s (buccal surface) Default placement for all cases — use when lower first molars have no large restorations or crowns
2nd choice Occlusal of upper molars Lower 6s have large restorations or crowns — move bite blocks to upper molars
3rd choice Occlusal of second molars Unable to place on upper first molars — use second molars as next option
Deep bite cases Palatal to upper 3s (U3) Severely deep bite cases where posterior bite blocks are insufficient — Dr. Akhavan to determine
📏 Height is critical: Build bite blocks only high enough for the patient to just clear their brackets when biting. Do not overbuild — excess height causes discomfort, TMJ strain, and speech difficulty. After placement, have patient bite down and verify bracket clearance before dismissal.
🧪 Bonding Protocol — Per-Tooth Steps
1
Etch Apply 37% phosphoric acid to the enamel surface.
↓
2
Rinse Rinse thoroughly for 10 seconds.
↓
3
Dry Air-dry until the enamel appears chalky white. Do not contaminate with saliva.
↓
4
Prime / Luting Agent Apply Assured Plus per manufacturer instructions.
↓
5
Apply Adhesive Apply adhesive to the bracket base.
↓
6
Seat Bracket Position bracket at correct height and angulation; remove excess composite.
↓
7
Cure Light-cure mesial and distal — 4 seconds each side.
🔬 Bonding Protocol by Surface Type
🦷 Enamel ✨ Porcelain ⚙️ Metal 🔵 Composite
Acid Etching
Acid etch? Yes Yes NO Yes
Type 37% Phosphoric Acid 5% Hydrofluoric Acid (HF) — 5% Hydrofluoric Acid (HF)
Working time 30 sec 60 sec — 60 sec
Rinse & Dry ✓ ✓ — ✓
Confirm roughness visually ✓ ✓ — ✓
Additional steps None
1 Porcelain Conditioner — 2 min
2 Air dry thoroughly
 —
1 Monobond Plus — 2 min
2 Air dry thoroughly
Primer / Luting Agent Assured Plus Assured Plus Assured Plus Assured Plus
Sand Blasting Etching
Sand blast? NO Yes Yes Yes
Confirm roughness visually — ✓ ✓ ✓
Rinse & Dry — ✓ ✓ ✓
Conditioner —
1 Porcelain Conditioner — 2 min
2 Air dry thoroughly
1 Monobond Plus — 2 min
2 Air dry thoroughly
1 Monobond Plus — 2 min
2 Air dry thoroughly
Primer / Luting Agent — Assured Plus Assured Plus Assured Plus
📌 Correct Bonding Sequence
Step 1 — U/L 6s Step 2 — Lower arch Step 3 — Upper arch Left side: 5 before 4
Patient's Right Patient's Left
Step 3 — Upper arch ←  ←  ←  ←  ←  ←  ←  ←  ←  ←
Upper
UR6
UR5
UR4
UR3
UR2
UR1
UL1
UL2
UL3
UL42nd
UL51st
UL6
Lower
LR6
LR5
LR4
LR3
LR2
LR1
LL1
LL2
LL3
LL42nd
LL51st
LL6
→  →  →  →  →  →  →  →  →  → Step 2 — Lower arch
1
Bond U/L 6s
Bond all four first molars first (UR6, UL6, LR6, LL6).
2
Lower arch: LR5 → LL5
Work across the lower arch from LR5 to LL5.
3
Upper arch: UR5 → UL5
Work across the upper arch from UR5 to UL5.
4
Left side: 5 before 4
On both left sides (UL & LL), always bond the 5 first, then the 4.

Time Management

  • Keep Dr. A chairside for 'Dr. Time' ONLY (10 min).
  • Bring the patient back and dismiss on time.

Patient Management

  • Keep the patient comfortable and informed throughout the procedure.
  • Do not exceed the scheduled appointment time.
  • Deliver post-op instructions and goodie bag info correctly and in a timely manner to the patient and/or RP — do not rush or drag it out. See SOP-CL-010: Patient Instructions by Appointment Type for the full braces bonding script.
  • Ensure the patient understands what the next appointment will be.
  • Enter notes in the chart before dismissing the patient.
InSmile™ Braces System — Clinical Protocol
SOP-CL-028  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Standard
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-028
System
InSmile™ Braces — Smartwire + Self-Ligating Brackets + IDB Trays
Wire Sequence
.014" → .017" → .020"

System Components

ComponentDescription
Smartwires™Shape-memory wires with interproximal (IP) loops that contour gingivally between brackets, and Lockets that engage the self-ligating brackets. Available in .014", .017", and .020".
Wire Progression.014" — initial leveling/aligning  ·  .017" — completing alignment + inter-arch mechanics  ·  .020" — expressing final tooth movements
BracketsSelf-ligating, four types: Lower Anterior, Upper Anterior, Small Posterior, Large Posterior. Secured via two Occlusal Tabs, a Gingival Tab, and an active Locking Spring.
IDB TraysThree segments per arch (one anterior, two posterior) for indirect bonding bracket placement.

Initial Records Requirements

  • ABO format photos.
  • Panoramic X-ray.
  • Intraoral scan — capture all tooth surfaces and export as .STL in occlusion to capture the true occlusal relationship.

Bonding — Tray & Tooth Preparation

⚠️ Work in low ambient light during tray prep — light exposure activates the primer/adhesive prematurely.

Tray Prep

  1. Apply a very thin layer of primer to the center of each bracket base — avoid contact with the tray material.
  2. Dispense adhesive using the Adhesive Fill Guide and pad with a microbrush to ensure 100% bracket base coverage.

Tooth Prep

  1. Clean all lingual surfaces with a prophy angle and pumice; rinse thoroughly.
  2. Isolate the working field using standard protocol — no cheek retractors.
  3. Air dry with a dry, moisture-free air source.
  4. Apply 37% phosphoric acid etch for 30–60 seconds, rinse 5 seconds, air dry until teeth appear chalky white.
  5. Apply a very thin layer of primer, air thin, and light cure 5 seconds per tooth.

Bonding — IDB Tray Delivery

  1. Seat each IDB tray segment starting from the lingual, rolling toward the labial. Apply light lingual and occlusal pressure.
  2. Light cure each bracket 5 seconds, then a second 5-second cure.
  3. Remove the tray by gently rolling it in an incisal-lingual-gingival motion.
▶ Video — Bonding Lower Arch
▶ Video — Bonding Upper Arch
▶ Video — Freehanding a Bracket
▶ Video — Increasing Patient Comfort with SoftFlow
▶ Video — Creating a Single Tooth Jig

Smartwire Insertion & Ligation

⚠️ Use Lingual Weingart pliers to grasp the Locket body. Never use Mathieu pliers on the IP loops — serrated tips cause micro-fractures.
  1. Deliver the Locket at a 20–30° angle.
  2. Place the tip of the Smartwire InGager on the Neck of the Locket — tip centered and parallel to the bracket's long axis.
  3. Press down at a 30° angle (gingival-slightly labial) to compress the Locking Spring until the Shoulders pass safely underneath the Occlusal Tabs.
▶ Video — Inserting a Smartwire
▶ Video — Securing a Mini Tab

Smartwire Disengagement

  • Posterior brackets: use the angled InGager tip.
  • Anterior brackets: use the straight InGager tip.
  • Line up the tip with the purchase point on the Neck of the Locket at a 20° buccolingual inclination. Push gingivally on the Neck to disengage.
▶ Video — Disengaging Smartwire with Plier
▶ Video — Disengaging Smartwire Using the InGager
📞 InSmile Braces Support  ·  Phone: 1-800-977-0744  ·  Email: support@insmilebraces.com
👤 Rep: Kristen Mikhail  ·  1 (773) 603-4433

Instrument Reprocessing — Smartwire InGager

  1. Clean: Instrument brush under warm running water (85°F–104°F). Ultrasonic cleaner with pH-neutral enzymatic detergent. Rinse thoroughly and submerge in disinfection solution.
  2. Sterilize (autoclave):
    • Gravity Displacement: 132°C for 5 min + 20-min dry time.
    • Pre-Vacuum: 132°C for 4 min + 20-min dry time.
Wire Placement & Cinching Protocol
SOP-CL-027  ·  Clinical Assistant
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-027
Applies To
All fixed braces wire placements (bonding & adjustment appointments)
Wire Types
Round NITI · Rectangular NITI · Rectangular SS/TMA

Round NITI Archwires — Size, Anneal & Cinch

⚠️ All round NITI archwires must be cinched distally. Anneal before cinching — un-annealed NITI will spring back or fracture. Anneal outside the mouth — never use a flame near the patient.

Step 1 — Intraoral Measurement & Marking

  1. Passively seat the new archwire into the bracket slots and molar tubes — do not ligate yet.
  2. Align the wire exactly with the patient's dental midline.
  3. Use a wax pencil or marker to mark the wire exactly where it exits the distal end of the terminal tube on both sides.

Step 2 — Extraoral Trimming

  1. Remove the archwire from the mouth.
  2. Using distal end cutters, snip the wire exactly 2 mm past the mark to ensure the correct tail length.

Step 3 — Extraoral Annealing

  1. Grip the wire firmly with a Weingart plier exactly on the marker line — the metal body acts as a heat sink, protecting the active portion of the wire from losing its temper.
  2. Pass the exposed 2 mm tail through the blue part of the flame for 1–2 seconds.
  3. Stop when the tip glows a dull cherry red.
  4. Allow to air-cool completely — the tip is now dead-soft. Repeat on the opposite side.

Step 4 — Ligation

  1. Re-insert the annealed wire into the patient's mouth.
  2. Ligate the archwire securely anterior to posterior using elastomeric ties, steel ligatures, or self-ligating doors.

Step 5 — The Final Cinch

  1. Use a dental mirror to retract the cheek for clear visibility.
  2. Using a Weingart or dedicated cinching plier, grip the dead-soft 2 mm tail.
  3. Bend the tail 45°–90° gingivally (toward the tissue) and slightly inward toward the tooth. Never bend outward toward the cheek.

Step 6 — Tactile "Finger Test"

  1. Run a gloved index finger lightly over the back of the terminal tube.
  2. If the wire catches the glove at all, use pliers to tuck the tail tighter against the base of the tube.

Rectangular Archwires — Crimpable Stops

📌 Rectangular wires (NITI, SS, or TMA) should not be cinched — the rectangular cross-section resists bending and can distort the wire. Instead, use 2 crimpable stops to prevent distal sliding and SOS. Crimpable stops also maintain the position of an open coil spring when one is in use.

Tool Selection

⚠️ Do not use a Weingart, Howe, distal end cutter, or bracket remover to crimp stops. These apply uneven pressure — the stop will spin or slide, and nicking the wire creates a stress fracture point. Always use a dedicated crimpable stop plier (small notch designed to wrap around both the stop and wire simultaneously).

Placement Protocol — The Double-Crimp Technique

  1. Seat the wire fully into all bracket slots and molar tubes.
  2. Position 2 crimpable stops — one mesial to each molar tube. Confirm placement with doctor if uncertain.
  3. Dry the wire with the air syringe at the stop location — saliva is a lubricant and is the leading cause of stops that slide post-appointment.
  4. Tack crimp — give each stop a light initial squeeze to hold it in place while you verify position and measurements.
  5. Primary crimp — place the plier's notch directly over the stop and squeeze firmly until both wings are fully flattened against the wire.
  6. 90° rotation crimp — rotate the plier 90° and give one more firm squeeze. This compresses the stop from all angles so it bites into the wire and cannot spin or shift.
  7. Verify the wire cannot slide mesially or distally after both stops are placed.
Archwire Progression Sequence
SOP-CL-008  ·  Clinical Assistant
Reference
▾

Advance to the next wire only when the current wire has become passive. Always confirm with Dr. A before advancing. Appointments are spaced based on the current phase.

Phase 1 — Initial Leveling  ·  8 weeks/appointment
1
.012 NiTi  ·  Severe crowding only — very flexible, light force
2
.014 or .016 NiTi  ·  Mild to moderate crowding — starting wire. Continue for 1–2 appointments at 8 weeks each.
Phase 2 — Transitional  ·  8 weeks
3
.018 Square NiTi  ·  Transitioning to rectangular wire — fills the slot progressively
Phase 3 — Working Stage  ·  8–10 weeks/appointment
4
16×22 NiTi or SS  ·  Working stage begins. Start elastics. Use powerchain to close spaces if indicated.
🦷 Deep bite present? Use 16×22 Reverse Curve or M arch wire instead of standard 16×22 — to express torque and prescription. Continue until bite opens adequately before advancing.

When the wire becomes passive (teeth fully expressed, no more tooth movement), schedule the Pan & Repo appointment.

📸 Pan & Repo Checkpoint
  • Take progress panoramic X-ray — evaluate root parallelism.
  • If no repositioning needed: advance directly to 16×22 Resolve (TMA) → enter Finishing phase.
  • If repositioning required: drop down to .016 for 8–10 weeks, then return to 16×22 TMA before entering Finishing phase.
5a
.016 NiTi (repo cases only)  ·  8–10 weeks, then back to 16×22 TMA
Phase 5 — Finishing & Detailing  ·  4–6 weeks/appointment
6
16×22 Resolve (TMA)  ·  Finishing and detailing. Appointments every 4–6 weeks. Continue until ready for debond.

Appointment Interval Summary

PhaseWire(s)Interval
Initial Leveling.012 / .014 / .016 NiTi8 weeks · 1–2 appointments
Transitional.018 Square NiTi8 weeks
Working Stage16×22 NiTi or SS8–10 weeks
Pan & Repo (checkpoint)Progress pano · repo if needed—
Repo (if needed).016 → 16×22 TMA8–10 weeks
Finishing & Detailing16×22 Resolve (TMA)4–6 weeks
Adjustment (Braces)
SOP-CL-002  ·  Clinical Assistant
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-002
Dr. Time
< 3 min
Edge Code(s)
600–620 (Wires/Adjustments)

Tray Setup

  • Set up the tray fully before seating the patient.

Case Presentation to Dr. A

  • Introduce the patient properly (first name, fun fact, etc.).
  • Prepare and present AWs and elastics.
  • Format the target date correctly (e.g., Target is 11/24).
  • Report broken appliances to the doctor promptly.

Time Management

  • Bring the patient back on time.
  • Dismiss the patient on time — do not let the appointment run over.
  • Keep Dr. chairside only for 'Dr. Time' (< 3 mins).

Procedural

  • Insert arch wires correctly per the treatment plan (TxP).
  • Trim arch wires to the proper length.
  • Correctly direct and size cinched wires.
  • Place crimpable stops correctly and ensure they are secure.
  • Place O-ties or power chains correctly per the plan.

Patient Management

  • Keep the patient comfortable throughout the procedure.
  • Record the patient's oral hygiene (OH) and compliance accurately.
  • Enter notes and photos in the chart before dismissing the patient.
If any standard was not met, document the challenge encountered and the steps you will take to improve in the comments field of the NLO evaluation form.
🦷
Elastic Prescription Guide
SOP-CL-014  ·  Orthodontist & Clinical Assistant
Clinical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-014
Category
Clinical Procedures
Performed By
Orthodontist (prescription) + Clinical Assistant (delivery & instruction)
Applies To
All patients prescribed intermaxillary elastics

Purpose

Standardize the selection, instruction, and documentation of orthodontic elastics to ensure correct force vectors, consistent patient education, and accurate chart records.

🏷️ Brand: NLO uses American Orthodontics (AO) elastics exclusively. AO elastics are named after animals — each size + force combination has a unique animal name (e.g., the 3/16" Medium is a specific animal, the 1/4" Heavy is another). Always refer to the elastic by its animal name when ordering from the supply catalog. The quick-reference table below lists our stocked combinations.

NLO Elastic Inventory — American Orthodontics

SizeForce GradeForce (oz)AO Animal NamePrimary Use
3/8"Medium4.5 ozTigerAs directed by Dr. Akhavan
3/16"Medium3.5 ozGorillaClass II/III — standard correction
3/16"Heavy6.5 ozTortoiseClass II/III — stronger correction, good compliance needed
3/16"X-Heavy8 ozCheetahClass II/III — maximum force, as directed by Dr. Akhavan only
1/4"Light2.5 ozFalconVertical / open bite, midline, or Class II with aligner hooks
1/4"Medium4.5 ozBald EagleVertical / open bite — standard
1/4"Heavy6.5 ozSea LionVertical / open bite — heavier correction
1/8"Medium4.5 ozGray WolfLight anterior force — as directed by Dr. Akhavan

Animal names and forces confirmed from supply cabinet labels. Verify Tiger (3/16" Light) and Gray Wolf (1/4" X-Heavy) labels if in doubt — confirm oz rating from packaging and update this table accordingly.

Elastic Configuration by Correction Goal

GoalConfigurationStarting Force Grade
Class II correctionUpper canine hook → lower 1st molar hook (triangle or straight run)Light (2.5 oz) → advance to Medium as tolerated
Class III correctionLower canine hook → upper 1st molar hook (anterior-to-posterior)Light (2.5 oz) → advance to Medium as tolerated
Vertical / open biteBox elastic: upper posterior → lower posterior (e.g., U6–U7 / L6–L7)Light or Medium (1/4")
Midline correctionDiagonal: upper canine → lower opposite canineLight (3/16" or 1/4") — unilateral
🚫 Power Chain Wire Rule: Do NOT place power chain on round wires or .018×.018 wire. Power chain may only be used on rectangular wires larger than .018×.018 (e.g., .019×.025 SS or .017×.025 SS). Using power chain on round or .018×.018 wire causes uncontrolled tipping and incisor flaring without adequate torque control.

D2 Distalizer Elastic Protocol

MalocclusionForce 1 (Month 1)Force 2 (Month 2+)AO Equivalent
Class II Force 1 — full time 22–24 hrs/day for 1 month Switch to Force 2 at 1-month mark; continue until super Class I achieved Force 1 ≈ Sea Lion (1/4" 6.5 oz)
Force 2 ≈ Cheetah (3/16" 8 oz)
Class III Force 1 — full time 22–24 hrs/day Remain on Force 1 only for entire treatment — never advance to Force 2 (joint risk + smaller lower canine roots) Force 1 ≈ Sea Lion (1/4" 6.5 oz)
📌 D2 Elastic Notes: Force 1 → Force 2 transition for Class II cases happens at exactly 1 month regardless of how much correction is visible — do not advance early. Target correction is to super Class I (¼ cusp overcorrection) before removing the appliance, as some relapse occurs post-removal. After removal, have the patient wear elastics at night for the same duration it took to achieve correction. Expected treatment time: Class II 3–6 months; Class III 4–5 months. Check appointments every 4–5 weeks.

D2 Non-Responders — Troubleshooting Protocol

If a patient is compliant but shows no meaningful correction after 3 months (including 2 months on Force 2), evaluate the following two causes before concluding failure:

CauseHow to IdentifySolution
Dense bone / heavy build Patient has a strong, heavy build with dense cortical bone; heavy occlusal forces resist tooth movement; not frequent but possible Double up Force 2 elastics at night only: if bonded to lower 7s → two Force 2 elastics at night; if bonded to lower 6s → one Force 1 + one Force 2 at night. Resolves movement in ~95% of cases.
Canines lodged in buccal plates Canine roots are in dense buccal cortical bone (little vascular supply) — visible on CBCT or suspected when canines appear displaced buccally; teeth move very slowly or not at all despite elastics Remove standard-length D2 appliance and replace with Shorty length (<20 mm) from 1st molar to 1st premolar. Shorter arm creates faster movement and gets teeth out of the buccal plate faster.
⚠️ Before assuming non-compliance: Always rule out the above two mechanical/biological causes first. Check that the hook configuration is correct, no bonds are lost, and the Sidekick hook on the lower arch is properly seated. Only after excluding these factors should compliance be the primary attribution.

When to Start Elastics

  • Braces patients: Elastics are typically started when canines and molars are fully bonded and arch leveling is sufficiently advanced (archwire ≥ .017×.025 SS or as prescribed).
  • Aligner patients: Elastics are started when aligner hooks or precision cuts are present on the current tray. Confirm the cut/hook is active — not just planned for a future tray.
  • Do not start elastics if anchor teeth have loose brackets, broken bonds, or inadequate hooks. Fix mechanical issues first.

Clinical Assistant — Elastic Delivery Protocol

  1. Pull the correct elastic pack (size and force as prescribed by Dr. Akhavan). Confirm the label matches the prescription exactly.
  2. Demonstrate the correct configuration to the patient using a model or in the patient's own mouth (gloved).
  3. Watch the patient place and remove the elastic independently at least twice before dismissal.
  4. Hand the patient two bags of elastics (or one full bag with at least 50 elastics). Elastics should be changed every 12 hours (morning and night) for consistent force.
  5. Give the patient the appropriate written elastic instruction card matching their configuration (Class II / Class III / Vertical / Midline). Keep a supply of printed instruction cards at the clinic station.
  6. Document in chart: elastic size, force, configuration, arm of hook used, patient demonstrated technique, quantity dispensed.
⚠️ Key Patient Instructions: Elastics must be worn 22–24 hours/day including while eating unless Dr. Akhavan specifies otherwise. Removing them only to brush and floss. Wearing them only at night produces very limited correction and extends treatment.

Elastic Compliance Check (Follow-up Visits)

  • At each appointment, evaluate occlusal change and ask about compliance before patient sits in chair.
  • If no meaningful correction: rule out wrong hook, wrong configuration, or lost hooks/brackets before attributing to non-compliance.
  • Document compliance level (full / partial / none) in visit note each time elastics are active.
  • Reinforce technique and motivation at every visit — show the patient their progress photos to boost engagement.

Documentation

  • Elastic prescription entered in chart at time of prescription: size, force, configuration, start date.
  • Each visit: elastic compliance noted, configuration confirmed or changed, quantity dispensed.
  • Changes in elastic prescription (e.g., switching from Class II to vertical) documented with clinical rationale.
🔧
Detailing Appointment (Braces)
SOP-CL-018  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Standard
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-018
Phase
Finishing & Detailing (Phase 5 — 16×22 Resolve/TMA)
Interval
Every 4–6 weeks
🦷 Assistant — Pre-Appointment Setup
  1. Seat patient and visually inspect all brackets, bands, and appliances for damage or breakage.
  2. Chart any broken or loosened appliances in the patient record.
  3. Remove all ties and archwires.
  4. Dismiss patient to brush teeth thoroughly.
🦷 Assistant — Post-Brushing Verification
⏱️ While the patient is brushing, the assistant should complete the following tray setup — do not wait until the patient returns.
  1. Lay out Tweed plier and arch forming plier on the tray.
  2. Remove the upper and lower archwires and lay them out on the tray. Label each wire with both arch (U / L) and side (R / L) so orientation is preserved for Dr. A.
  3. Ensure wire sequence and any additional materials are ready and accessible.
  4. When patient returns, confirm thorough brushing — no food debris present.
  5. Call doctor to treatment area.
👨‍⚕️ Doctor — Wire Assessment & Bending
  1. Review patient presentation from assistant.
  2. Assess whether wire bends are indicated based on tooth movement and treatment objectives.
  3. Place any necessary bends in the wire using appropriate pliers.
  4. Document wire adjustments in Edge.
  5. Release from chair.
🦷 Assistant — Appointment Completion
  1. Reinsert upper and lower wires into brackets.
  2. Tie all brackets securely with ligatures or elastic ties.
  3. Replace upper and lower elastics as indicated.
  4. Schedule follow-up detailing or evaluation appointment 4–6 weeks out.
  5. Dismiss patient.
✨
Debonding (Braces)
SOP-CL-003  ·  Orthodontist & Clinical Assistant
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-003
Appt Length
60–90 min
Follow-up
Retainer delivery — 2 business days later (SOP-CL-003B)
Edge Code(s)
702–712 (Debond/Removals)
📅 Two-appointment workflow: Debonding and retainer/whitening tray delivery are separate appointments. After debonding, a final scan is taken and retainers + custom whitening trays are fabricated. The patient returns 2 business days later for delivery. Schedule the retainer delivery appointment before the patient leaves today.

Tray Setup

  • Debonding instrument: dull ligature cutter with color marking (NLO-specific tool — do not use a standard bracket remover).
  • High-speed handpiece + 4 burs (all Komet USA) — set up in order of use:
    #BurCodePurpose
    1Finishing burH48L.FG.012Remove residual adhesive from buccal surfaces only
    2Football carbide burH379.FG.018Remove bite blocks/turbos; also used to remove adhesive from occlusal & lingual surfaces
    3Damon bur8379.FG.023Damon bracket adhesive removal
    4Football diamond burKometEnameloplasty of U/L anterior teeth

    📦 All burs are stocked in the tilt-out bins in the sterilization area.

  • UV/black light for flash detection.
  • Scaler, prophy cup and paste.
  • Scanner (iTero or Allied Star) — warmed up and tip prepared: Allied Star tip autoclaved (up to 180 cycles); iTero tip is single-use disposable.

Steps

  1. Remove brackets using the dull ligature cutter (color-marked debonding instrument) — squeeze gingivally and incisally to break the bond. Do not remove archwires or ligatures/modules separately; brackets are removed with archwire and ligatures still in place.
  2. Once all brackets are off, remove the archwire.
  3. Bur #1 — Finishing bur (H48L.FG.012): Remove residual adhesive from buccal surfaces only using light, sweeping strokes — do not over-reduce enamel. Clinical assistant holds UV/black light over each tooth while Dr. Akhavan inspects for remaining composite flash; re-address any flagged areas before moving on.
  4. Bur #2 — Football carbide bur (H379.FG.018): Remove adhesive from occlusal and lingual surfaces. Also used to remove bite blocks/turbos. Rinse and suction the patient thoroughly after this step.
  5. Bur #4 — Football diamond bur: Perform enameloplasty on upper and lower anterior teeth as indicated.
  6. Polish all surfaces with prophy cup and paste.
  7. Take post-treatment intraoral photos (full series) and panoramic X-ray.
  8. Take final scan (iTero or Allied Star) — full arch, confirm scan quality. This scan is used to fabricate the removable retainers and custom whitening trays.
  9. 🏆 Hall of Fame Wall: Have the patient sign the Hall of Fame wall — this is our wall where every patient signs after completing their treatment. Make it a moment to celebrate!
  10. Schedule the Retainer & Whitening Tray Delivery appointment 2 business days from today before the patient leaves. Explain to the patient that their retainers and whitening trays will be ready at that visit.
Submit scan for retainer and whitening tray fabrication immediately after appointment.
Clear Aligner Delivery & Management
SOP-CL-004  ·  Orthodontist & Clinical Assistant
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-004
Wear Time
20–22 hrs/day
Change Interval
Per Rx (7–14 days)
Edge Code(s)
301–312 (Invisalign) · 316–327 (Next Level)

Initial Delivery

  1. Verify aligner case matches patient name, case number, and tooth count.
  2. Place attachments per ClinCheck/treatment plan using attachment template trays.
  3. Perform IPR per staging instructions using approved strips/discs; measure with gauge.
  4. Seat tray 1 (or 1–2 as prescribed); verify full seating with chewies.
  5. Educate patient: wear time, removal for eating/drinking (except water), cleaning, chewie use, switching schedule.

Monitoring Visits

  1. Confirm wear compliance and current tray number.
  2. Assess tracking — if gap between tray and teeth >1mm, do not advance.
  3. Perform any staged IPR; add/modify attachments as needed.
  4. Provide next series of trays; document in chart.
🔷
Aligner Attachment Bonding (NLA / Invisalign)
SOP-CL-009  ·  Clinical Assistant
Standard
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-009
Edge Code(s)
301–302, 307–308 (Invisalign) · 316–317, 322–323 (Next Level)

Required Supplies

  • NOLA / cheek retractor
  • 37% phosphoric acid etchant
  • Assure Plus primer
  • Tetric EvoCream composite (for filling attachment trays)
  • Curing light
  • Handpiece (for flash cleanup)
  • Blue flashlight (to detect excess composite flash)

Steps

  1. Isolate with NOLA/cheek retractor and dry teeth thoroughly.
  2. Etch upper and lower arches — 30 seconds each arch.
  3. Rinse thoroughly with water and high-speed suction.
  4. Air dry completely — you should see a white chalky surface on all etched teeth.
  5. Apply Assure Plus primer (paint on like a fingernail — do not scrub); lightly air dry.
  6. Fill the attachment trays with Tetric EvoCream composite.
  7. Seat trays onto teeth; lightly press against the front teeth to ensure full seating.
  8. Cure teeth with trays still in place.
  9. Remove trays.
  10. Use a handpiece to clean up excess composite flash around each attachment — use the blue flashlight to identify all flash.
💡 Always keep the last set of trays as a backup. Never discard old trays until the next set is confirmed to fit. If an attachment comes off between visits, teeth will still track — it can wait until the next appointment.
🔄
Aligner Refinement Protocol
SOP-CL-013  ·  Orthodontist & Clinical Assistant
Clinical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-013
Category
Clinical Procedures
Performed By
Orthodontist (assessment) + Clinical Assistant (scans)
Applies To
All clear aligner patients requiring mid-course correction
Edge Code(s)
305–308 (Invisalign Refinement) · 320–323 (Next Level Refinement)

Purpose

Define the criteria for identifying poor aligner tracking, the workflow for submitting a refinement case, and responsibilities at each step.

Tracking Evaluation — When to Refinement

At each aligner check appointment, the orthodontist assesses tracking by visual inspection and seating. Refinement is indicated when:

  • Patient cannot seat the current aligner fully — gap between aligner and one or more teeth ≥ 1 mm (assessed with chewie pressure applied).
  • Staging goals are not being met (e.g., tooth has not moved as planned after 2+ consecutive aligner sets).
  • Attachments are lost and tooth is tracking behind the virtual model.
  • Patient reports aligner feels "loose immediately" upon switching to a new set — indicates the prior set was not tracked properly.
  • Treatment is at or near the end of the original series and significant tooth movement remains.

Pre-Refinement Checklist

  1. Confirm patient is wearing their last prescribed aligner — they must be in their final aligner (or as directed by Dr. Akhavan) before scanning.
  2. Review compliance: ask how many hours/day the patient wears aligners. Document compliance level in chart.
  3. Identify and address all tracking issues before scanning — bond replacement attachments, IPR if needed, take correction impressions if required.
  4. Take pre-refinement intraoral photos: full series (R/L buccal, frontal retracted, upper/lower occlusal). These document the starting point for the refinement case.
  5. Take intraoral scan (iTero or Allied Star). Confirm scan quality: no voids, complete arch capture including second molars.
📐 Scan Timing: Scans for refinement must be taken with the patient out of aligners for at least 2 hours before scanning to allow teeth to settle into their true position.

Submission Workflow

  1. Upload scans (or impressions) to the aligner manufacturer portal (e.g., Invisalign Doctor Site or equivalent).
  2. Indicate in the prescription: treatment goals, tooth movements still needed, any modifications (IPR, attachments, staging preferences).
  3. Select refinement type: Full Refinement (complete re-planning from current position) or Limited Refinement (targeted correction of specific teeth).
  4. Submit and note submission date in chart. Set a recall task in Asana/Edge for plan review in 2–3 weeks.
  5. When the refinement plan arrives, Dr. Akhavan reviews the ClinCheck/virtual setup before approving for manufacture.

Refinement Aligner Delivery Appointment

  1. Verify patient is still in their last aligner from the original series — aligners must not be abandoned between plan and delivery.
  2. Review the new aligner series with the patient: total stages, expected wear schedule, any IPR or attachment changes.
  3. Place/replace attachments per the new template tray. Remove old attachments that are no longer in the new plan.
  4. Deliver Tray 1 of the refinement series. Confirm fit and patient's ability to seat and remove the aligner.
  5. Provide written wear instructions. Remind patient: 22 hours/day minimum — compliance is the most common cause of tracking failure and re-refinement.
  6. Document in chart: refinement start date, total stages, attachment changes, patient compliance note.

Documentation

  • Pre-refinement photos and scan date recorded in visit note.
  • Submission confirmation (case number / submission date) logged in chart.
  • Attachment template tray number and any IPR performed noted.
  • Asana task created for plan review and delivery follow-up.
💎
Retainer & Whitening Tray Delivery
SOP-CL-003B  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-003B
Timing
2 business days after debond — SOP-CL-003 Debonding
Appt Length
30 min
Edge Code(s)
425–433 (Retainer Deliveries)
🚨 Retainers must be ready at the front desk before 8:00 AM on the day of pickup. Enter completion into Milestones in Edge once ready.

Before the Appointment

  • Confirm removable retainers are fabricated, trimmed, and polished — verify fit on model.
  • Confirm custom whitening trays are fabricated and trimmed.
  • Place retainers and whitening trays at the front desk before 8:00 AM — patient picks up at the front desk.
  • Enter retainer readiness into Milestones in Edge.
  • If a bonded lingual retainer is planned, notify the clinical team — that portion requires a chair visit per SOP-CL-012.

Front Desk — Retainer Pickup

  1. Greet the patient and confirm their name and date of debond.
  2. Hand over the retainer case with removable retainers and whitening trays.
  3. Review the retention wear schedule with the patient:
    • Days 1–5: Full-time wear — remove only to eat and brush.
    • Day 6 onward: Nighttime wear only — indefinitely to maintain the result.
  4. Demonstrate proper retainer insertion and removal. Watch patient perform independently before dismissal.
  5. Provide written retention and whitening tray care instructions.
  6. Schedule the retention check at 3 months — this is the only scheduled retention check.
Mark retainer delivery complete in Milestones in Edge once the patient has picked up.
💎
Retention Protocol — Removable & Fixed Retainers
SOP-CL-012  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-012
Included
1 set removable retainers
Material
Zendura A (default)
Machine
Ministar S
Turnaround
2 business days
Edge Code(s)
501–503 (Retention Checks)

Wear Schedule (All Patients)

  • Days 1–5: Full-time wear — remove only to eat and brush. This allows teeth to fully settle into their new positions.
  • Day 6 onward: Nighttime wear only — for life, or as long as the patient wants to maintain their result.
  • Every patient receives 1 set of removable retainers included with treatment.

Fixed (Bonded/Permanent) Retainer — Retainium Wire

A fixed retainer may be recommended by Dr. Akhavan in certain clinical situations (e.g., significant spacing, midline diastema, lower anterior crowding, high relapse risk). If recommended, it should be placed — but it does not replace or change the removable retainer protocol. The patient still gets and wears their removable retainer as described above.

NLO uses Retainium wire (.027" × .011" ribbon arch) by Reliance Orthodontics for all direct-bond fixed retainers. Retainium is nickel-free and MRI compatible — document this in the patient chart. For the full chairside bonding protocol, see SOP-CL-019 — Retainium Direct Bond Retainer.
▶ Reference — Retainer Types Compared

Retainer Types — Hawley vs. Essix/Trutain

HawleyEssix / Trutain (Zendura A)
MaterialMetal wire + acrylicThermoplastic clear retainer material
Tooth coverageDoes not cover entire tooth surface — labial bow sits facially, acrylic contacts palatallyCovers entire tooth surface — full occlusal and interproximal contact
DurabilityVery durable; adjustableMay wear or discolor over time; not adjustable
AestheticsVisible metal bowNearly invisible
FeeAdditional fee if patient requestsIncluded in treatment fee (default)
▶ Reference — Zendura A & Ministar S Settings

Zendura A Material & Ministar S Settings

  • Zendura A is a dual-laminate retainer material — a rigid outer layer bonded to a soft inner layer — providing durability with patient comfort. It is our standard material for all in-house Essix-style retainers.
  • Fabricate on the Ministar S pressure thermoforming machine — select Program 132 for Zendura A.
  • Place one sheet of material per cycle. Once the packaging is opened, the sheet must be used within 15 minutes — do not leave open sheets sitting out.
  • Allow full cool-down before removing from model to prevent distortion.
  • See SOP-EQ-004 — MiniStar S for full machine operation details.

Trimline Standards

Trimline accuracy directly affects fit, comfort, and tissue health. Every retainer must be checked against these standards before delivery.
  • Facial: No more than 1 mm past the gingival margin — trim to just scallop the tissue line.
  • Lingual: Trim to the CEJ (cemento-enamel junction) — do not extend onto root surface or cover excess soft tissue.
  • Smooth all edges after trimming — no sharp or rough borders.

Edentulous Areas / Missing Teeth

  • Any edentulous space or missing tooth on the model must be blocked out before thermoforming to prevent the retainer from locking into the undercut.
  • Use Ivery Liquid Dam (flowable block-out) to fill the space on the model. Do not use block-out putty — it does not adapt well and may create voids.
  • Allow the block-out to cure fully before thermoforming.

Fabrication Workflow & Asana

  1. Scan the patient for retainers (upper and/or lower as indicated); confirm scan quality before dismissing.
  2. Add the patient to Asana under the appropriate retainer fabrication column immediately after scanning. Do not wait until the next day.
  3. Print model, block out edentulous areas if needed (Ivery Liquid Dam), thermoform with Ministar S using Zendura A.
  4. Trim to standard trimlines (facial ≤1 mm past gingival margin, lingual at CEJ); smooth all edges.
  5. Quality-check: verify full seating on model, no voids, no sharp edges.
  6. Standard turnaround: 2 business days from scan date.
  7. Patient receives the 3D-printed model along with their retainers at delivery.

Fixed Retainer Placement Protocol (Retainium Wire)

  1. Check occlusion first — wipe the target teeth clean with a 2×2 gauze, air-dry for 10 seconds, place articulating paper, and have the patient bite down multiple times. Confirm there is adequate clearance for the wire in centric and lateral excursions before proceeding. If clearance is insufficient, consult Dr. Akhavan.
  2. Place floss through all contact areas in the span of the planned wire — these will be used to stabilize the wire during bonding.
  3. Heat-treat the wire to shape and anneal it to the lingual contour of the teeth. Confirm passive fit against the lingual surfaces before bonding.
  4. Position the wire in the mouth and secure it with the floss — loop the floss around the wire at each contact point to hold it firmly against the teeth while you bond.
  5. Using an instrument, burnish the wire against the teeth to ensure full contact and correct positioning along the lingual surfaces.
  6. Etch the lingual surfaces of all teeth the wire contacts — standard etch technique, rinse thoroughly, and air-dry.
  7. Apply primer to the etched surfaces; gently air-thin.
  8. Apply LCR (light-cured resin / flowable composite) over the wire at each contact point, covering the wire fully. Keep resin contained — avoid excess flash onto gingival tissue.
  9. Light cure each bonded point per manufacturer's recommended time.
  10. Pull the stabilizing floss facially and toward the gums to remove — do not pull occlusally or lingually, which risks dislodging the freshly bonded wire.
  11. Check occlusion again with articulating paper. Remove any high spots.
  12. Flash check: Use a black light / UV light to illuminate the bonded area. Any composite flash on adjacent tooth surfaces or soft tissue will fluoresce and become clearly visible. If flash is detected, use a football carbide bur in a handpiece to carefully remove it. Recheck with UV light until the area is clean.
  13. Verify bond integrity by gently tapping on each bonded point. Document wire span (e.g., "lower 3–3 fixed retainer placed") in the chart.
🧲
Retainium Direct Bond Retainer — Chairside Placement
SOP-CL-019  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Clinical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-019
Wire
Retainium .027" × .011" ribbon arch (Reliance Orthodontics)
Notes
Nickel-free · MRI compatible — document in patient record
⚠️ Wire must be fully passive before bonding. Never bond an active wire. Re-adapt on a model or typodont until the wire lies passively against each tooth.

Supplies

  • Retainium wire (.027" × .011" ribbon arch)
  • Prophy paste
  • EtchMaster or microetcher (50 µm aluminum oxide)
  • 37% phosphoric acid etch
  • Assure® or Assure Plus® Universal Bonding Resin
  • FlowTain® flowable composite or Reliance L.C.R. light-cure retainer paste
  • Dental floss (wire stabilization)
  • Light cure unit + protective shield

Step-by-Step Protocol

  1. Wire Adaptation — Pre-adapt the Retainium wire to the lingual surfaces of the anteriors (canine to canine, or as directed). Work on a study model if available. The wire must lie passively against each tooth with no active forces. Trim to length.
  2. Sandblast the Wire — Lightly microetch the bonding contact areas of the wire with 50 µm aluminum oxide using a sandblaster or EtchMaster. This roughens the surface and significantly improves composite adhesion.
  3. Prophy the Teeth — Polish lingual surfaces with prophy paste. Rinse and dry thoroughly.
  4. Microetch Enamel — Microetch lingual surfaces using EtchMaster or equivalent (50 µm aluminum oxide) in a continuous sweeping motion for 3–5 seconds per tooth. Rinse and dry.
  5. Acid Etch — Apply 37% phosphoric acid to lingual surfaces. Etch for 30 seconds, then rinse thoroughly (at least 15 seconds) and dry completely. Enamel should appear frosty/chalky white.
  6. Apply Bonding Resin — Apply one coat of Assure® or Assure Plus® to all bonding surfaces. Lightly air-thin with a gentle air syringe. Do not light cure yet.
  7. Position Wire with Floss — Loop dental floss through the interproximal contacts of each anterior tooth (centrals and laterals). Slide the Retainium wire under the floss loops and gently pull the floss to hold the wire snug against the lingual surfaces. This keeps the wire stable without an assistant holding it.
  8. Apply Composite — Working one or two teeth at a time:
    • Extrude a small amount of FlowTain® flowable composite (or L.C.R. light-cure paste) over the wire on each tooth.
    • Fully encapsulate the wire — composite must cover the wire completely.
    • Keep each pad small and smooth — avoid excess bulk to reduce occlusal interference and ease patient hygiene.
  9. Light Cure — Cure each bonded pad for 20 seconds per tooth, working systematically from one end to the other.
  10. Finish and Check
    • Remove the floss ligatures.
    • Check occlusion — patient bites together; confirm no interference on the retainer.
    • Trim and polish any excess composite with a finishing bur or disc.
    • Confirm the wire is fully encapsulated and no sharp edges are exposed.

Clinical Tips

  • If the wire is not sitting passively after adaptation, re-adapt before bonding — never bond an active wire.
  • Small bonded pads reduce occlusal interference and make hygiene easier for the patient long-term.
  • Review floss threading technique with the patient at this appointment.
  • Retainium is nickel-free and MRI compatible — note this in the patient chart.
↔️
Palatal Expander Delivery & Activation
SOP-CL-005  ·  Orthodontist & Clinical Assistant
Standard
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-005
Expansion Rate
Per Rx
Edge Code(s)
403–408 (Expander Delivery) · 504 (Check/Activate)
ℹ️ NLO uses 3D printed (laser-sintered) bands — not traditional metal bands cemented with glass ionomer. Bands are bonded to the teeth using Reliance Assure Plus primer and Band Lok adhesive.

Tray Setup

  • Nola cheek retractor
  • Microbrushes and cotton angles
  • Dappen dish or well
  • Reliance gel etch
  • Reliance Assure Plus + spatula
  • Band Lok
  • Motor with prophy angle
  • Light cure unit
  • Light-tight container (for prepped bands)

Band Placement Protocol

  1. Try-in: Seat the 3D printed band on the molar to confirm fit before proceeding. Band should seat fully with no rocking.
  2. Tooth prep: Prophy all band surfaces (buccal and lingual) with prophy angle. Place Nola cheek retractor for access.
  3. Etch: Apply Reliance gel etch to all buccal and lingual surfaces of the molar for 30 seconds.
  4. Rinse & dry: Suction thoroughly, rinse, and air dry completely.
  5. Prime the band: Apply Assure Plus to the inside of the band. Air dry, then light cure. Place prepped band in a light-tight container immediately to prevent premature cure.
  6. Prime the tooth: Apply Assure Plus directly to the tooth surface. Air dry — do not light cure this step.
  7. Cement: Fully coat the inside of each band with Band Lok. Seat the band on the tooth and remove excess adhesive with a cotton roll or microbrush.
  8. Cure: Light cure for 45 seconds using slow, circular motions to ensure full coverage.
  9. Final rinse: Rinse patient thoroughly. Confirm band is fully seated and not impinging on soft tissue.
  10. Confirm expander is not impinging on soft tissue or preventing normal occlusion.

Patient/Parent Instructions

  • Demonstrate activation with key; turn toward the back of the throat.
  • Record the prescribed number of turns per day and start date.
  • Advise: mild soreness, temporary lisp, and midline diastema expected.
  • Instruct on rinsing after meals; no sticky or hard foods.
⚠ If the patient/parent is unable to reliably activate, consider in-office activation only or reeducate. Document number of turns completed at each visit.
🦷
Herbst Appliance Delivery
SOP-CL-017  ·  Orthodontist & Clinical Assistant
Standard
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-017
Appliance
Herbst (fixed functional — mandibular advancement)
Band System
Rolo Band + Band Lok
Teeth Involved
Upper and lower first molars (U6s and L6s)
ℹ️ The Herbst appliance is a fixed Class II corrector that positions the mandible forward. It is cemented to the upper and lower first molars using Rolo bands and Band Lok. No primer is used at any step — etch directly and apply Band Lok to the bands.

Tray Setup

  • Herbst appliance (confirm U and L sides are correctly labeled)
  • Band Lok adhesive — stored under the box when not in use to block light
  • Gel etch + microbrushes
  • Cotton rolls
  • Scaler
  • Light cure unit + curing light protector

Steps

  1. Appliance prep:
    • If the appliance arrives assembled: remove the arms from the lower components before proceeding.
    • If the upper components are not connected to each other (no expander or TPA linking them): place them on the tray and clearly mark R and L so they are not mixed up during cementation.
  2. Try-in: Seat the upper components on the upper first molars and the lower components on the lower first molars. Confirm full seating on all four molars — no rocking, no excess space.
  3. Isolate: Place cheek retractors for access and isolation.
    4. ⬆️ UPPER — Complete all steps before moving to lower
  4. Etch upper: Apply gel etch to the buccal and lingual surfaces of both upper first molars. Hold for 30 seconds.
  5. Rinse & dry upper: Suction thoroughly, rinse, and air dry completely.
  6. Cement upper: Apply Band Lok inside each upper band. Keep bands face-down under the box to block light. Seat upper components firmly on U6s.
  7. Remove excess — upper: Use cotton rolls to remove excess cement immediately after seating.
  8. Initial cure — upper: Light cure for 4 seconds to tack.
  9. Clean gingival margin — upper: Use a scaler to remove remaining excess cement along the gingival border.
  10. Final cure — upper: Light cure for a full 10 seconds.
    11. ⬇️ LOWER — Repeat same sequence on lower
  11. Etch lower: Apply gel etch to the buccal and lingual surfaces of both lower first molars. Hold for 30 seconds.
  12. Rinse & dry lower: Suction thoroughly, rinse, and air dry completely.
  13. Cement lower: Apply Band Lok inside each lower band. Keep bands face-down under the box to block light. Seat lower components firmly on L6s.
  14. Remove excess — lower: Use cotton rolls to remove excess cement immediately after seating.
  15. Initial cure — lower: Light cure for 4 seconds to tack.
  16. Clean gingival margin — lower: Use a scaler to remove remaining excess cement along the gingival border.
  17. Final cure — lower: Light cure for a full 10 seconds.
    18. 🔧 ASSEMBLE & CHECK
  18. Attach Herbst mechanism: Connect the arms between upper and lower components bilaterally. Confirm pivot joints seat fully and the mechanism opens and closes without binding.
  19. Check function: Have patient open and close. Confirm:
    • Mandible advances symmetrically — no lateral shift
    • No soft tissue impingement (cheeks, tongue, gingiva)
    • Patient can open to at least 35–40 mm without the mechanism disengaging
    • Occlusion in protrusive position is as planned
  20. Final rinse: Rinse patient thoroughly.

Patient & Parent Instructions

  • Expect soreness and jaw fatigue for 5–7 days — normal as muscles adjust to the new jaw position.
  • Soft diet for 1 week: no hard, crunchy, or sticky foods for the first week; soft foods ongoing to protect the appliance.
  • No chewing gum, hard candies, ice, or foods that require wide opening (e.g., large sandwiches, apples).
  • Clean around bands and the Herbst mechanism with a soft toothbrush after every meal. Use a proxy brush or water flosser to clean under the mechanism.
  • If a pivot pin or screw comes loose: call the office immediately — do not wait for the next scheduled appointment.
  • If the appliance causes a sore spot on the cheek or gum: call the office for a same-week adjustment appointment.

Scheduling

  • Schedule follow-up 4–6 weeks out for the first adjustment and tissue check.
  • Typical Herbst treatment duration: 9–12 months of full-time wear.
🔩
MSE Delivery & TAD Placement
SOP-CL-020  ·  Orthodontist & Clinical Assistant
Clinical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-020
Lab
Specialty Orthodontic Lab
Anchorage
2 palatal TADs (mini-implants)
Imaging Required
Intraoral scan only (no CBCT needed)
Activation
1 turn/day (per lab Rx)
Edge Code(s)
403–408 (Expander Delivery) · 513 (TADs)
ℹ️ MSE (Specialty Orthodontic Lab): Intraoral scan only — no CBCT required. Submit the scan with your prescription and Specialty plans the TAD positions.

MARPE (Partner Dental Studios): CBCT is required and is typically obtained by the referring OS or Perio before the appliance is submitted. Confirm CBCT is on file before ordering.

Pre-Submission (Before Lab Order)

  1. Take an intraoral scan of the upper arch. No CBCT is needed for the MSE from Specialty Orthodontic Lab.
  2. Submit the scan and prescription to Specialty Orthodontic Lab. The lab plans the TAD positions and fabricates the appliance with built-in guide holes for the 2 TADs.
  3. The MSE appliance arrives with built-in guide holes — the appliance itself acts as the surgical guide for TAD insertion.
  4. Confirm appliance receipt and verify patient name, case number, and molar band sizes before scheduling the delivery appointment.

Tray Setup

  • MSE appliance from Specialty Lab
  • Band Lok adhesive
  • Cheek retractors & tongue retractor
  • Cotton rolls & gauze
  • Topical anesthetic (compounded gel — see SOP-CL-016)
  • Local anesthetic syringe, carpule, needle (27 ga short)
  • TAD driver (hand or low-speed handpiece)
  • TAD mini-implants (provided by Specialty Lab with appliance)
  • Activation key (included with appliance — give to patient)
  • Light cure unit + protective shield
  • Dental mirror, explorer, college pliers

Try-In

  1. Seat the MSE appliance on the upper molars without cement to verify fit.
  2. Check that molar bands are fully seated, passive, and the appliance body rests close to (but not pressing on) the palatal mucosa.
  3. Confirm the TAD guide holes are accessible and positioned correctly over the mid-palatal area.
  4. If fit is inadequate, do not cement — contact Specialty Lab.

Step 1 — Topical Anesthetic

  1. Apply compounded topical anesthetic gel to the palate at planned TAD sites using the 3-cycle protocol — see SOP-CL-016. Apply each coat at 0.5 mm thickness.

Step 2 — Appliance Cementation

ℹ️ NLO uses 3D printed bands bonded with Reliance Assure Plus primer and Band Lok adhesive. Follow the same band bonding protocol as the RPE — see SOP-CL-005 — Palatal Expander Delivery for full step-by-step.
  1. Tooth prep: Prophy buccal and lingual surfaces of the upper first molars.
  2. Etch: Apply Reliance gel etch to all buccal and lingual surfaces for 30 seconds. Rinse thoroughly and air dry completely.
  3. Prime the bands: Apply Assure Plus to the inside of each molar band. Air dry, then light cure. Store in a light-tight container immediately.
  4. Prime the teeth: Apply Assure Plus directly to the tooth surfaces. Air dry — do not light cure this step.
  5. Cement: Fully coat the inside of each band with Band Lok. Seat the MSE appliance onto both molars simultaneously and remove excess adhesive.
  6. Cure: Light cure for 45 seconds using slow circular motions. Confirm full seating before proceeding.

Step 3 — Local Anesthetic Injection

  1. Administer palatal infiltration (27 ga short needle, 2% lidocaine 1:100,000 epi) at each planned TAD site.
  2. Wait 3–5 minutes for full anesthetic effect. Confirm profound anesthesia with a sharp explorer before proceeding to TAD insertion.

Step 4 — TAD Insertion

⚠️ The appliance guide holes dictate exact TAD position — do not deviate from the guide. Insert all TADs perpendicular to the palatal surface through the guide holes.
  1. Confirm anesthesia is still profound. Dry the palate with gauze.
  2. Place the TAD driver through the first guide hole. Apply steady, controlled rotational pressure — do not force. The TAD threads into cortical palatal bone.
  3. Insert until the TAD head is flush with the guide hole and seated firmly. Do not overtighten.
  4. Repeat for the second TAD site (2 TADs total).
  5. Visually confirm both TADs are fully seated and stable. Check that neither is mobile.
  6. Gently irrigate the palate with saline or water to clear any debris.

Post-Placement Check

  • Verify appliance is stable — no rocking or movement of the molar bands.
  • Check occlusion — patient should be able to close without the appliance causing a premature contact or significant open bite (a small temporary open bite in the anteriors is expected and normal).
  • Take a post-placement photo of the palate documenting TAD positions and appliance seating.
  • Document TAD length, brand, and insertion sites in the patient chart.

Patient Instructions — Activation

  • Activate the expander 1 turn per day using the key provided. Each full turn = 0.25 mm of expansion.
  • Best time to turn: before bed, with patient lying down for better palatal visibility. A second person (parent/partner) should assist.
  • Insert the key into the expansion hole and push toward the throat until the next hole becomes visible. Remove the key carefully.
  • A midline diastema opening between the upper front teeth is expected and is a positive sign that the suture is opening — reassure the patient.
  • Mild soreness and pressure in the face, nose, and teeth after each turn is normal for the first few days. OTC pain relievers (ibuprofen) can help.
  • Stop turning and call the office if: severe or persistent pain, nosebleeds that do not stop, a TAD feels loose, or the appliance shifts.
  • Avoid hard and sticky foods. Brush carefully around the appliance and TADs twice daily with a soft toothbrush. Use a proxy brush or water flosser around the TAD heads.
  • Do not attempt to turn the key more than once per day unless specifically instructed by Dr. Akhavan.

Monitoring & Follow-Up

VisitTimingPurpose
1-week check7 days post-deliveryVerify TAD stability, tissue health, patient compliance with turning, address soreness concerns
Progress checkEvery 4–6 weeksAssess expansion progress, confirm diastema, evaluate TAD stability
Goal expansion reachedPer RxStop activation. Do not remove appliance — consolidation phase begins
Consolidation6–9 monthsAppliance stays passive in place while bone fills the expanded suture. More time required for adults
Appliance removalAfter consolidationRemove TADs and decement bands. Fabricate retainer immediately
📋 Diastema closure: The midline diastema that opens during expansion will close naturally on its own once activation stops — do not bond or close it artificially during the consolidation phase.
🔄
Phase I Protocol — Expander & 2×6 Braces
SOP-CL-007  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Standard
▾
407
Visit 1 — Scan & Records

When Patient Is in the Chair

  • Confirm patient and review medical history / allergies — flag any updates in Edge.
  • Take clinical photos (full series: frontal, lateral, intraoral).
  • Take panoramic X-ray if not already on file or if updated pano is indicated.
  • Have Dr. A complete the clinical exam and confirm treatment plan (U RPE + L Schwartz).
  • Ask patient / parent their color preference for the L Schwartz acrylic.

Scanning

  • Scan upper arch — include the full palate (critical for RPE fabrication).
  • Scan lower arch.
  • Take a bite registration scan (MIP / CO bite).
  • Verify both arches are complete and free of voids before dismissing patient.

Appliance Submission — Complete Before End of Day

🚨 Submit the Tally form the same day as the scan. Do not wait until the next day.
  • Follow SOP-CL-006 — Appliance Submission to complete the Tally form.
  • Include: patient name, appliances ordered (U RPE + L Schwartz), color choice, scan date, and any special instructions from Dr. A.
📋 Open Appliance Submission Form tally.so/r/3EpQdr

Scheduling

  • Schedule NV 2 weeks + 1 day out — code 407 (Delivery visit).
  • Confirm appointment with patient / parent before they leave.
↓
407
Visit 2 — Delivery
  • Deliver U RPE and L Schwartz.
  • U RPE: turn one time per night for 28 turns.
  • L Sch: turn one time per week.
  • Schedule NV 4 weeks out — code 212.
↓
212
Visit 3 — Check Expansion & Bond U C–C
  • If expansion looks good: tie off RPE and bond U C–C.
  • Place U seg wire NT.
  • L Sch: begin turning two times per week — not two consecutive days (e.g., Sunday & Wednesday).
  • Schedule NV 6 weeks out — code 601.
↓
601
Visit 4 — U Adj & L Expansion Check
  • Check L expansion; continue L Sch at two turns per week.
  • U arch should still be in seg wire — advance to .016 NT if able.
  • Schedule NV 6 weeks out — code 216.
↓
216
Visit 5 — D/C RPE & L Sch, Bond Full 2×6
  • D/C U RPE; bond U 6's.
  • D/C L Sch; bond L 2×6; BB L 6's.
  • U wire: MUST be continuous wire extended to U 6's — bumper tubes posterior, annealed and cinched.
  • OCS D U 2's if needed with SS tie U 2's and U C's.
  • L wire: start in seg wire — .012 NT, .014 NT, or .016 NT.
  • Schedule NV 6–8 weeks out — code 601.
↓
601
Visit 6 — U Consolidation & L Leveling
  • U ADJ: consolidate U 2–2 and make room for U 3's.
  • L wire: continue L NT seg if still crowded.
  • When L arch levels to L 16×22 NT or 16×22 SS: extend to L 6's with bumper tubes, annealed and cinched.
  • OCS D L 2's if needed with SS tie L 2's and L C's.
  • Schedule NV 6–8 weeks out — code 601.
↓
601
Visit 7+ — Ongoing Adjustments
  • Continue same mechanics.
  • See patient every 4–6 weeks for adj until DEB.
  • Monitor for Phase II treatment.
📤
Appliance Submission — Scan & Tally Form
SOP-CL-006  ·  Clinical Assistant
Critical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-006
Edge Code(s)
151ST–158 (Scans/Records/Impressions)

Scanning Steps

  1. Scan the upper arch — include the full palate.
  2. Scan the lower arch.
  3. Verify both scans are complete, accurate, and free of voids before proceeding.

Submit the Appliance Order

Open the Tally submission form and complete all required fields:

📋 Open Appliance Submission Form tally.so/r/3EpQdr
🚨 The Tally form must be completed the same day the patient was scanned. Do not wait until the next day.
🦴
Decoronation — Ankylosed Primary Molar (Growing Patient)
SOP-CL-025  ·  Orthodontist
Critical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-025
Decision Maker
Orthodontist
Referral
Oral Surgeon / Pediatric Dentist (for decoronation procedure)
Applies To
Growing patients with infraoccluded, potentially ankylosed primary molars

When to Apply This Protocol

Consider decoronation when all three of the following are present:

  • Patient is still in active skeletal growth
  • Primary molar is in infraocclusion (submerging below the occlusal plane)
  • Ankylosis is confirmed or strongly suspected (percussion test, radiographic loss of PDL space, progressive submergence)
▶ Background — Why Decoronation Instead of Extraction

Why Decoronation Instead of Extraction

In a growing patient, an ankylosed primary molar left in place or simply extracted will result in significant alveolar bone loss at that site. Decoronation preserves the vital root structure, which acts as a scaffold for bone — allowing normal alveolar growth to continue over and around it.

💡 Vital root > RCT-treated root. If the primary molar has had a pulpotomy or pulpectomy (root canal treated), the preserved root offers less biological benefit. A vital root provides superior bone preservation outcomes. Factor in root vitality when counseling the family.

Referral & Surgical Target

  1. Refer to an oral surgeon or pediatric dentist experienced in decoronation.
  2. Specify in the referral: decoronation to 2 mm below the crestal bone level.
  3. The crown is removed; the root is left in place intentionally. The site is closed primarily.
  4. Over time, bone grows over the retained root fragment as the patient develops.

Post-Decoronation Monitoring

  • Monitor alveolar bone height radiographically at regular intervals.
  • Assess the site prior to any planned implant or restorative work in that region.
  • Coordinate with the restorative dentist regarding long-term space management and eventual implant timing (typically after growth is complete).
⚠️ Extraction without decoronation in a growing patient can lead to severe vertical bone deficiency at the site, complicating future implant placement. When ankylosis and growth are both present, decoronation is the preferred intervention.
🔀
Canine Substitution — Bracket Selection Protocol
SOP-CL-026  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Critical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-026
Staff
Clinical Assistant (setup) · Orthodontist (verify)
Reference
Kravitz et al., JCO August 2017
Applies To
Cases with canine substitution for a missing or extracted lateral incisor
🚨 The most common error in canine substitution is inadequate palatal torque of the relocated canine. Bracket selection is the primary tool for correcting this — verify with the doctor before pulling brackets.

NLO Bracket Protocol

Tooth Bracket Why
Canine (moving into lateral incisor position) Same-side U1 (maxillary central incisor) Highest palatal root torque (+17°, 4° tip — MBT). Counteracts canine root prominence and compensates for torque loss at the wire-slot interface. Flat base — enameloplasty required prior to bonding.
First Bicuspid (moving into canine position) U3 bracket (same side) Canine bracket prescription positions the bicuspid root to simulate the canine emergence angle.
First Molar Lower contralateral molar bracket Compensates for mesial tip and torque adjustments needed when the arch is shifted.
▶ Doctor Reference — Torque & Wire Details

Why U1 on the Canine

The canine's natural root torque is labially directed. When relocated into the lateral incisor position, this torque must be overcorrected palatally. The central incisor bracket (+17° torque) provides significantly more palatal root torque than a lateral incisor bracket (+10°) or a flipped canine bracket (+7°). Bracket position can be adjusted for an additional ~4° of distal root tip if needed.
