Torque Loss — The "1 to 5" Rule

For every .001" of vertical play between slot and archwire, approximately 5° of torque is lost. With our .018" slot, a .017×.025" wire leaves only .001" play (~5° loss). Advance to .017×.025" on the substitution canine.

WireSlotTorque Loss
.017 × .025".018"~5°← NLO
.019 × .025".022"10–15°

Archwire Material

At high torque angles (>24°), SS yields ~2× the torque of beta-Ti and ~3× NiTi. For isolated torquing bends at the canine, beta-titanium is preferred to avoid excessive torsional force.

Labial Step-Out Bend & Canine Extrusion (Detailing)

A labial step-out bend adds palatal root torque and reduces occlusal trauma on lower incisors while improving the contact point between the central and relocated canine. Some torque loss is also recovered by extruding the canine labially during detailing — improves gingival architecture but may cause lower incisor contact; manage with the step-out bend.

Setup Checklist

  • Confirm with doctor which side(s) are receiving canine substitution before pulling brackets.
  • Plan and complete enameloplasty on the canine before bonding the U1 bracket (flat base requires reshaping).
  • Separate substitution brackets from standard arch brackets before bonding to avoid mix-ups.
  • Document all substitution bracket assignments in the patient chart.
⚡
Gingivectomy / Gingival Contouring with Electrosurge
SOP-CL-011  ·  Orthodontist (Procedure) & Clinical Assistant (Setup/Assist)
Clinical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-011
Device
Sensimatic 700SE
Mode
Fully Filtered CUT or BLEND 1
Electrode
Loop (#113) or Fine Needle (#103)
Anesthesia
Local (topical + infiltration)
Sterile Ref
SOP-EQ-001 + SOP-EQ-002
Edge Code(s)
510 (Laser Recontouring) · 511 (Soft Tissue) · 512 (Frenectomy)
⚠️ Pacemaker / Cardiac Device: Do NOT use electrosurgery on any patient with a cardiac pacemaker or implanted electronic device unless cleared in writing by the patient's cardiologist. Electrosurgical current may interfere with device function.

Indications

  • Bracket exposure: Remove hyperplastic tissue obscuring unerupted or partially erupted teeth prior to bonding.
  • Gingival troughing: Create a sulcular trough around a bracket/band for clean bond or band placement on tissue-covered surfaces.
  • Crown lengthening (minor): Contour uneven gingival margins for aesthetic finishing.
  • Frenectomy (labial/lingual): Reduce frenum attachment inhibiting tooth movement or closure of a diastema.
  • Operculectomy: Remove operculum over a partially erupted molar.

Contraindications

  • Active infection or acute periodontal abscess in the operative area.
  • Inadequate attached gingiva (<2 mm) — refer to periodontist.
  • Patient on anticoagulants or with uncontrolled bleeding disorders — consult physician first.
  • Alveolar bone within ≤3 mm of planned incision depth — risk of osseous damage.
  • Pregnant patients in first or third trimester — elective procedures should be deferred.

Tray Setup

  • Sensimatic 700SE unit, power cord, foot pedal, and dispersive electrode (grounding pad).
  • Sterile electrode tip(s): Loop electrode (#113) for soft tissue excision; Fine Needle (#103) for troughing/detail work. Electrode and handpiece sterilized per SOP-EQ-001 (M11 Pouches cycle 132°C/5 min).
  • Topical anesthetic: Compounded gel — 3% Lidocaine / 12.5% Tetracaine / 3% Phenylephrine (Rx from Curative Medicine Compounding Pharmacy, Gainesville, FL). Supply: cotton tip applicators.
  • Anesthetic syringe with aspirating tip, carpule (e.g., 2% lidocaine 1:100,000 epi), and needle (27 ga short).
  • Chlorhexidine (CHX) rinse and cotton tip applicators — for pre-procedural site prep and post-excision irrigation.
  • Sterile gauze 2×2, suction tip, mouth mirror, explorer.
  • Bonding tray (if bonding follows immediately after tissue removal).
  • Wet gauze or damp cotton roll to wipe electrode tip during procedure.

Patient Preparation

  1. Review medical history; confirm no pacemaker, implanted cardiac device, or absolute contraindications.
  2. Explain procedure to patient and guardian; answer questions; obtain verbal assent and document in chart.
  3. Seat patient semi-supine; place protective eyewear on patient.
  4. 📷 Take pre-procedure intraoral photos — capture the operative site clearly (retracted view + close-up of the target teeth/tissue). These become the baseline record. Upload to patient chart before proceeding.
  5. Apply compounded topical anesthetic gel (3% Lidocaine / 12.5% Tetracaine / 3% Phenylephrine) to operative site using the 3-cycle protocol — see SOP-CL-016: Compound Topical Anesthetic Protocol. Note: this preparation provides stronger and faster onset than standard benzocaine formulations.
  6. Administer local anesthetic infiltration; wait 3–5 minutes for full effect. Confirm profound anesthesia before proceeding.
  7. Attach dispersive electrode (grounding pad) to patient's upper arm or thigh per manufacturer instructions. Ensure firm, full skin contact.

Unit Setup (Sensimatic 700SE)

  1. Connect grounding pad cable, handpiece, and foot pedal to the unit.
  2. Power on. Select Fully Filtered (CUT) mode for clean tissue excision with minimal char, or BLEND 1 for simultaneous hemostasis.
  3. Start at power setting 3–4. Adjust up (max 6–7) only if tissue resistance is high. Lower settings = less thermal damage.
  4. Attach the appropriate sterile electrode to the handpiece.
  5. Test activation briefly on a gauze pad to confirm the unit is operational before tissue contact.
🔥 Electrode Rule: Keep the electrode in continuous, smooth motion during activation. Never hold the electrode stationary on tissue — this causes deep thermal necrosis, delayed healing, and possible osseous damage. If the tissue begins to turn white or char, reduce power and speed up the stroke.

Procedure Steps

  1. Isolate the field with gauze and suction; keep the area as dry as possible (moisture disperses current and reduces efficiency).
  2. Activate electrode with foot pedal before contact with tissue. Do not touch tissue first and then activate — this causes sticking and charring.
  3. Perform excision with a single smooth stroke following the planned incision line. For troughing, trace the sulcular margin with the needle electrode in a controlled sweep.
  4. Wipe the electrode tip on damp gauze between each pass to remove carbonized debris. A coated tip increases resistance and reduces cutting efficiency.
  5. Evaluate hemostasis — electrosurgery typically provides excellent hemostasis. If bleeding persists, switch to COAG mode briefly with light pressure, or apply a hemostatic agent.
  6. Assess tissue margins — confirm adequate tooth structure or bracket access is now exposed. Additional passes may be made after a brief pause to allow heat dissipation.
  7. Remove excised tissue with a curette or gauze. Inspect the wound margin.
  8. If bonding immediately: Allow the site to stabilize for 1–2 minutes; rinse copiously with water; proceed to bonding protocol (SOP-CL-001). Keep the area dry during bond procedure.
  9. 📷 Take post-procedure intraoral photos — same angles as pre-op (retracted view + close-up). Capture immediately after hemostasis and before patient dismissal. Upload and attach to today's chart entry alongside the pre-op images.

Post-Procedure Instructions (Patient)

  • Mild soreness is normal for 2–5 days as the site heals.
  • Take OTC pain reliever (ibuprofen or acetaminophen) as directed; avoid aspirin.
  • Rinse with warm salt water (½ tsp salt in 8 oz water) 2×/day starting the day after the procedure.
  • Avoid hard, spicy, or sharp foods near the area for 3–5 days.
  • Maintain gentle brushing; avoid vigorous rinsing or disturbing the site for 24 hours.
  • Call the office if: excessive bleeding that doesn't stop with light pressure, severe swelling, fever, or pus.
  • Tissue typically heals fully in 7–14 days. Follow-up as needed.

Post-Procedure Unit Care

  • Power off the unit; disconnect the handpiece and grounding pad.
  • Wipe the unit exterior with a disinfectant wipe (CaviWipes or equivalent); do not get liquid inside the unit housing.
  • Clean the handpiece and used electrode per SOP-EQ-001: inspect electrode tip for carbon buildup; clean with fine wire brush; sterilize in pouch using M11 Pouches cycle (132°C / 270°F, 5 min). Allow 15-min cool-down before removing.
  • Discard any single-use electrodes per sharps protocol.
  • Log the procedure in the unit maintenance log: date, operator, power setting used, and any observations.

Documentation (Chart Entry)

  • 📷 Pre- and post-procedure intraoral photos attached to today's visit note (required for all cases).
  • Tooth/teeth treated and location (facial / lingual / interdental).
  • Type of procedure: gingivectomy / troughing / frenectomy / operculectomy.
  • Anesthesia: topical (compounded 3% Lidocaine / 12.5% Tetracaine / 3% Phenylephrine — Curative Medicine Pharmacy, Gainesville) + infiltration type, dose, and lot number.
  • Power setting and electrode type used.
  • Patient response and hemostasis status.
  • Post-op instructions provided (verbal + written).

Follow-Up

Next Visit
2 weeks
Visit Type
Post-op evaluation
Appt Code
501
  • Schedule the post-op evaluation appointment before patient leaves — 2 weeks from the procedure date. Use appointment code 501 (pink) in Edge.
  • At the 2-week visit: assess tissue healing, confirm no infection or excessive scarring, take follow-up intraoral photo of the site, and compare to pre/post-op images from the procedure visit.
  • If healing is inadequate or tissue has re-grown, consult with Dr. Akhavan for a re-treatment plan.
✨
ZOOM Whitening Protocol
SOP-CL-015  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Clinical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-015
Category
Clinical Procedures
Performed By
Clinical Assistant (setup) + Orthodontist (supervision)
Edge Code(s)
517 (ZOOM Whitening)
Applies To
Patients at or near debond, or as standalone whitening appointment

Purpose

Ensure safe, effective, and consistent delivery of in-office ZOOM whitening with proper patient screening, tissue protection, and post-treatment care instructions.

Indications & Timing

  • Whitening is best performed after debond — brackets and adhesive removed, teeth professionally polished.
  • May be offered as a standalone appointment for existing patients who complete aligner treatment or as an add-on service.
  • Minimum age: 16 years (parental consent required for minors).
  • Teeth must be free of active decay, exposed dentin, cracked enamel, or open margins on restorations — screen before scheduling.

Contraindications

  • Active caries, open cavities, or failing restorations in anterior teeth.
  • Exposed root surfaces or significant gingival recession in anterior region.
  • Pregnancy or breastfeeding — defer until completed.
  • Known hypersensitivity to peroxide.
  • Veneers, crowns, or bonding on anterior teeth — whitening will not lighten restorations; advise patient before proceeding.
  • Patients under 16 years — not recommended.

Tray Setup & Supplies

  • ZOOM whitening lamp and light guide (verify lamp operational; wipe light guide with alcohol wipe).
  • ZOOM whitening gel syringes (check expiration date).
  • Opalescence or equivalent desensitizing gel (post-treatment syringe to send home).
  • Cheek retractor / lip retractor (dry field essential for gel placement).
  • Liquid dam (Ivery Liquid Dam or equivalent) — for gingival barrier. Light-cured; protect papillae and free gingival margin completely.
  • Protective eyewear for patient and operator.
  • Gauze, cotton rolls, saliva ejector.
  • Shade tab / VITA shade guide — document pre-treatment shade.

Patient Preparation

  1. Review medical history; confirm no contraindications. Document pre-treatment shade in chart (shade guide + photo).
  2. 📷 Take pre-whitening smile photo — retracted frontal smile with shade tab visible. Upload to chart before starting.
  3. Seat patient; place protective eyewear. Explain the procedure and what to expect (mild sensitivity during and after is normal).
  4. Insert lip/cheek retractor to fully expose the gingival margin around all anterior teeth to be treated.
  5. Dry the gingival tissue thoroughly with gauze. Apply liquid dam to cover gingival margin and papillae — light-cure per manufacturer instructions. Inspect coverage; re-apply any gaps.

Whitening Procedure (ZOOM Standard Protocol)

  1. Apply ZOOM whitening gel to facial surfaces of upper and lower anteriors (as indicated). Keep gel away from the gingival dam margins.
  2. Position ZOOM lamp approximately 1–2 cm from the teeth per lamp guidelines. Activate light.
  3. Standard treatment: 3 × 15-minute sessions with the ZOOM lamp. Remove gel with suction/gauze between each session; reapply fresh gel for next session.
  4. After each session, check patient comfort — mild tingling or sensitivity is expected; sharp pain or burning is a signal to stop and reassess.
  5. After the final session, remove all gel thoroughly with suction and gauze. Rinse well.
  6. Remove liquid dam using an explorer or scaler — inspect tissue for any irritation.
  7. Remove lip retractor. Have patient rinse. Inspect soft tissue for any signs of chemical burn — if present, apply vitamin E oil or chlorhexidine gel and monitor.
⚡ Sensitivity Management: If the patient reports significant sensitivity during treatment, pause the session. Apply desensitizing gel (potassium nitrate / fluoride) directly to the teeth for 5 minutes, then reassess before continuing. Do not push through severe sensitivity — document and consider a shorter protocol for future visits.

Post-Treatment

  1. 📷 Take post-whitening smile photo with the same shade tab — same angle as pre-op. Upload to chart alongside pre-treatment image.
  2. Document final shade achieved. Compare to pre-treatment shade in chart entry.
  3. Send patient home with desensitizing gel and instructions to use it for the next 2–3 days if sensitivity persists.

Post-Treatment Patient Instructions

  • White diet for 48 hours after treatment — avoid coffee, tea, red wine, berries, tomato sauce, or any deeply pigmented foods and drinks. Teeth are temporarily more porous post-whitening.
  • Avoid tobacco products for at least 48 hours.
  • Use desensitizing toothpaste (e.g., Sensodyne) for 1–2 weeks post-treatment.
  • Results typically last 6–18 months depending on diet and hygiene habits; touch-up trays or repeat sessions can maintain results.
  • Existing restorations (crowns, veneers, bonding) will not change color — they may appear darker against newly whitened natural teeth. Discuss timing of restoration replacement with the patient's restorative dentist if needed.

Documentation

  • Pre- and post-whitening photos attached to visit note (required).
  • Pre-treatment and post-treatment shade documented.
  • Number of sessions completed, any sensitivity events, and patient tolerance noted.
  • Desensitizing gel dispensed and post-op instructions provided — documented in chart.
💉
Compound Topical Anesthetic Protocol
SOP-CL-016  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Clinical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-CL-016
Applies To
TAD placement · Gingivectomy · Tissue uncovering
Total Time
~9 minutes (3 cycles × 3 min)
Edge Code(s)
513 (TADs) · 510–512 (Soft Tissue Procedures)
🚫 Do NOT apply topical directly from the syringe. Dispensing straight from the syringe causes the gel to spread uncontrolled across the tissue and reduces effectiveness. Always apply with a q-tip only. Apply each coat at approximately 0.5 mm thickness — a thin, even layer is more effective than a thick glob.

Purpose

Maximize soft tissue anesthesia before TAD placement, gingivectomy, or tissue uncovering using compound topical anesthetic applied in three timed cycles. Proper application ensures tissue is adequately anesthetized and minimizes patient discomfort.

Supplies

  • Compound topical anesthetic gel
  • Q-tips (one fresh q-tip per application cycle)
  • Cheek retractors
  • Cotton rolls
  • Air syringe

Protocol — 3-Cycle Application

⏱ Do NOT leave the patient during any cycle. Stay chairside for the full 3 minutes each cycle — monitor patient comfort and ensure the cotton rolls remain in place.
  1. Place cheek retractors to isolate the area and improve access.
  2. Place cotton rolls on each side of the target area to isolate the site and keep it dry.
  3. Dry the tissue thoroughly with the air syringe — topical anesthetic does not penetrate wet tissue effectively.
  4. Apply topical to the target area using a fresh q-tip in an even layer approximately 0.5 mm thick. Do not dispense directly from the syringe.
  5. Wait exactly 3 minutes. Remain chairside. Do not leave.
  6. Wipe away the topical with the cotton rolls. Rinse and dry the tissue with air.
  7. Repeat steps 3–6 two more times for a total of 3 full application cycles.
CycleStepsWait
1Dry → Apply 0.5 mm layer (q-tip) → Stay chairside3 min
2Wipe → Rinse → Dry → Apply 0.5 mm layer (q-tip) → Stay chairside3 min
3Wipe → Rinse → Dry → Apply 0.5 mm layer (q-tip) → Stay chairside3 min

Expected Result

  • After 3 cycles the tissue should appear visibly blanched — this confirms adequate topical penetration and the site is ready to proceed.
  • If blanching is not achieved after 3 cycles, notify Dr. Akhavan before proceeding with the procedure.
🦠 Infection Control 4 procedures ▾
🤲
Hand Hygiene & PPE Standards
SOP-IC-001  ·  All Clinical Staff
Critical
▾

When to Perform Hand Hygiene

  • Before and after each patient contact
  • Before donning and after removing gloves
  • After touching potentially contaminated surfaces or equipment
  • After removing mask or protective eyewear

Technique

Use ABHR (alcohol-based hand rub) for at least 20 seconds, covering all surfaces. Use soap and water when hands are visibly soiled.

PPE Requirements — Orthodontic Procedures

Gloves
All patient contact
Mask
All intraoral procedures
Eye protection
All procedures with aerosol or debris
Gown
When splash/spatter risk present
♻️
Instrument Sterilization Protocol
SOP-IC-002  ·  Sterilization Technician / All Clinical Staff
Critical
▾

Sterilization Method by Instrument Type

Instrument TypeMethodNotes
Metal instruments (pliers, scalers, brackets, etc.)Steam autoclave (M11)Pouch, label, and run full cycle — see SOP-EQ-002
Plastic instruments (mirrors, plastic-handled tools, heat-sensitive items)Glutaraldehyde solution (cold soak)Cannot withstand autoclave heat — submerge per manufacturer's contact time; rinse thoroughly before use
⚠️ Glutaraldehyde is a chemical sterilant/high-level disinfectant. Always wear gloves and eye protection when handling. Work in a ventilated area. Follow the solution manufacturer's recommended soak time for sterilization vs. high-level disinfection.

Steps — Metal Instrument Reprocessing (Autoclave)

  1. Transport: Place contaminated instruments in a closed container immediately; never carry loose.
  2. Ultrasonic Cleaner: Submerge instruments in ultrasonic cleaner with ultrasonic cleaner solution. Run for 15 minutes.
  3. Rinse: Remove instruments and rinse thoroughly under water.
  4. Dry: Dry instruments completely before packaging.
  5. Package: Place in sterile pouches and load into the autoclave.
  6. Sterilize: Run autoclave cycle per SOP-EQ-002.
  7. Store: Store pouches in a clean, dry area once cycle is complete.

Steps — Plastic Instrument Reprocessing (Glutaraldehyde Cold Soak)

  1. Don gloves and eye protection before handling glutaraldehyde solution.
  2. Thoroughly clean instrument of all visible debris before soaking — glutaraldehyde cannot penetrate biofilm.
  3. Fully submerge plastic instrument in glutaraldehyde solution. Ensure no air pockets.
  4. Soak for the manufacturer-specified contact time (sterilization requires longer contact than high-level disinfection — confirm time on solution label).
  5. Remove with sterile forceps; rinse thoroughly with sterile water before use on patient.
  6. Allow to air dry in a clean area before storage.

Quality Monitoring

  • Chemical indicator (Class 5 or 6) inside every pouch
  • Biological spore test — run weekly; document results and log
  • If spore test fails: remove all items from service; investigate; re-run
🚨 Never use an instrument that is not verified sterile. If pouch integrity is compromised (torn, wet, or broken seal) — reprocess before use.
🧼
Operatory Turnover & Surface Disinfection
SOP-IC-003  ·  Clinical Assistant
Critical
▾

After Each Patient

  1. Don gloves; remove all barriers (chair headrest, light handles, bracket tray, drawer pulls).
  2. Dispose of single-use items; place contaminated instruments in container for reprocessing.
  3. Wipe all non-barrier surfaces with an EPA-registered intermediate-level disinfectant wipe (e.g., CaviWipes); allow full contact time (typically 3 minutes).
  4. Apply fresh barriers to all contact surfaces.
  5. Set up for next patient; replace disposable supplies (cups, suction tips, gloves).
Contact time matters — wipe must remain visibly wet for the manufacturer-specified time to achieve disinfection. If it dries too quickly, rewipe.
🛡️
OSHA Clinical Guidelines — PPE & Chair Barriers
SOP-IC-004  ·  All Clinical Staff
Critical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-IC-004
Staff
All Clinical Staff
Authority
OSHA Bloodborne Pathogens Standard

Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

PPE ItemRequirementNotes
Nitrile Gloves All patient contact Single use only. Change between every patient — never re-use or sanitize and reuse.
Face Mask All intraoral procedures Replace when visibly soiled or wet.
Protective Eyewear All procedures with aerosol or debris Sanitize after each appointment. Do not swap between team members without sanitizing first.
Lab Jacket Messy procedures (bonding, IDB, etc.) Required when splash or splatter risk is present.

Chair Barriers — Replace Between Every Patient

The following barriers must be removed after each patient and replaced with fresh ones before the next:

  • Headrest cover
  • Light handle covers (both arms)
  • Mouse barrier
  • Air/water syringe covers
🚨 Never seat a new patient without first replacing all chair barriers. Barrier replacement is not optional — it is a required infection control step between every appointment.
🚨 Orthodontic Emergencies 4 procedures ▾
💥
Broken or Loose Bracket
SOP-EM-001  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Urgent
▾
SOP ID
SOP-EM-001
Edge Code(s)
800–802 (Comfort Visit)

Phone Triage

  • Is the bracket causing pain or embedded in soft tissue? → See same day.
  • Is the next appointment within 1–2 weeks? → Typically can wait.
  • Is it a key tooth (e.g., rotating tooth, open bite closure)? → Prioritize sooner.

In-Office Steps

  1. Remove the archwire from the affected area; remove loose bracket.
  2. Polish residual adhesive from enamel.
  3. Re-etch, prime, and rebond per bonding protocol (SOP-CL-001).
  4. Reseat archwire; verify ligature/module secure.
  5. Document tooth number, bracket type replaced, and cause if identified.
⚠ Review diet and oral hygiene habits. Multiple repeated bracket failures on the same tooth may indicate parafunction or non-compliance — discuss with patient/parent.
📌
Poking or Loose Wire
SOP-EM-002  ·  Clinical Assistant
Urgent
▾
SOP ID
SOP-EM-002
Edge Code(s)
800–802 (Comfort Visit)

Phone Advice for Patient

  • Apply orthodontic wax to the end of the wire for immediate relief.
  • Do not attempt to cut the wire themselves with household scissors.
  • If wire has shifted and is poking into the gum or cheek severely — call for same-day appointment.

In-Office Steps

  1. Identify the source: distal end, loose tie-back, or displaced wire.
  2. If distal end: bend the end down with a ligature director or crimp with distal end cutter.
  3. If wire has slipped: re-center the wire; verify both ends are tucked or cinched.
  4. If a segment of wire has come out of a bracket: re-ligate; check other ligatures.
  5. Confirm patient has no ulceration or soft tissue damage; apply Orabase or wax if needed.
😬
Lost or Broken Retainer
SOP-EM-003  ·  Treatment Coordinator & Clinical Assistant
Urgent
▾
SOP ID
SOP-EM-003
Edge Code(s)
804–805 (Repair/Replace Fixed Retainer) · 425–429 (Replacement Retainer)

Assessment Questions

  • How long has the retainer been missing/broken?
  • Is there visible relapse or tooth movement?
  • Is a bonded wire loose or has it detached?

Steps

  1. Schedule patient promptly — the longer without retention, the greater the relapse risk.
  2. Assess for tooth movement at the appointment; compare to post-treatment records.
  3. If no movement: take new scan/impression and fabricate replacement retainer.
  4. If movement has occurred: discuss retreatment options with orthodontist before fabricating retainer.
  5. Repair or replace bonded wire if detached — clean surface, re-etch, bond per protocol.
⚠ Document retainer loss, amount of time without retention, and any observed relapse. Note whether replacement is covered under the original treatment fee.
❤️
In-Office Medical Emergency Response
SOP-EM-004  ·  All Staff
Critical
▾
🚨 All staff must know the location of the emergency kit and AED. Review this SOP at least annually and after any emergency event.

Vasovagal Syncope (Fainting) — Most Common

  1. Recline patient; elevate legs above heart level.
  2. Loosen restrictive clothing; ensure airway is open.
  3. Apply cool damp cloth to forehead; monitor vitals.
  4. Do not leave patient alone; document and notify orthodontist.

Cardiac Arrest / Unresponsiveness

  1. Call 911 immediately.
  2. Assign someone to retrieve AED; begin CPR (30:2 ratio, 100–120/min).
  3. Apply AED pads and follow voice prompts.
  4. Continue until EMS arrives; do not stop CPR unless patient regains pulse or EMS takes over.

Anaphylaxis

  1. Administer epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) IM to outer thigh — this is first-line.
  2. Call 911 immediately.
  3. Lay patient supine; monitor airway and vitals.
🧰 Emergency Kit: Cabinet above the sink in sterilization  ·  🫀 AED: Sterilization area  ·  🚨 Emergency Contact: 911
🔬 Records & Imaging 3 procedures ▾
☢️
Panoramic Radiograph Prescription & Safety Policy
SOP-RC-001  ·  Radiograph-certified staff only
Critical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-RC-001
Core Principle
ALARA — As Low As Reasonably Achievable
Schedule Basis
Clinical indication only — no calendar intervals
Shielding Policy
No lead apron or thyroid collar (2026 ADA guideline)
⚠️ Radiographs are never prescribed on a fixed calendar interval. Every exposure must be individually justified by clinical examination, developmental stage, and a specific diagnostic need. Clinical staff may not take a panoramic image without documented doctor authorization.

Clinical Indications for Panoramic Imaging

A — Initial Case Assessment (Pre-Treatment)

  • Evaluate dental and skeletal development.
  • Assess root morphology, bone health, and presence of pathology.
  • Identify missing, supernumerary, or impacted teeth.

B — Mid-Treatment Evaluation

  • Root paralleling — assess root position prior to major repositioning or detailing (typically requested at transition to heavier finishing wires).
  • Pathology or complications — unexpected tooth mobility, suspected apical root resorption, or delayed eruption of transitional teeth.
  • Mechanics reassessment — evaluate progress of complex movements such as bringing an impacted canine into the arch.

C — Post-Treatment & Retention Phase

  • Pre-debond — verify final root alignment before appliance removal.
  • Third molar assessment — evaluate development, trajectory, and extraction indications (typically late adolescence).

Patient Safety & Shielding Protocol (2026 ADA Update)

⚠️ No physical shielding is used at NLO. Thyroid collars and lead abdominal aprons are strictly contraindicated — they provide no measurable benefit with modern digital imaging and beam collimation, and can interfere with the primary beam causing unnecessary retakes.
  • Our office utilizes advanced digital imaging and proper beam collimation, which produce negligible scatter radiation.
  • Staff must be prepared to confidently explain to patients and parents that removal of lead aprons is an evidence-based safety upgrade per the latest ADA guidelines.

Documentation Requirements

📋 Every image requires chart documentation before it is taken.
  • Record the specific clinical justification (e.g., "evaluate root parallelism prior to repositioning") in the patient's chart before exposure.
  • Images will not be taken solely to satisfy insurance frequency limitations or requests.
  • If a patient declines imaging, document informed refusal and the discussion of deferred-imaging risks.
🖥️
Intraoral Scanning Protocol
SOP-RC-002  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Standard
▾
SOP ID
SOP-RC-002
Scanning Path
O-L-B — Occlusal → Lingual → Buccal
Arch Order
Lower arch first, then upper, then bite

Scanner Tip Protocol

ScannerTip TypeProtocol
Allied Star (AS)AutoclavableAutoclave after each patient — tips are rated for up to 180 autoclave cycles. Do not discard after single use.
iTeroDisposableUse a new disposable tip for each patient. Do not reuse or autoclave iTero tips.

Standard Scanning Path — The "O-L-B" Strategy

📌 Always use a smooth, continuous path — stopping and restarting in the same area creates overlapping data and stitching errors. Start with the lower arch; it is generally easier to tolerate and helps the patient acclimate before scanning the palate.

A — Lower Arch (Mandible)

  1. Occlusal: Start on the occlusal surface of the terminal molar on one side. Slowly move across all occlusal surfaces to the opposite terminal molar — this builds the "backbone" of the scan.
  2. Lingual: Roll the scanner 45° to the lingual. Starting from the terminal molar, sweep back around to the starting side. Capture the lingual gingival margin.
  3. Buccal: Roll the scanner to the buccal. Sweep from the starting molar all the way around to the opposite side.

B — Upper Arch (Maxilla)

  1. Occlusal: Repeat the occlusal sweep from terminal molar to terminal molar.
  2. Palatal/Lingual: Roll 45° and sweep the palatal surfaces.
  3. Palate (expanders/appliances): After teeth are captured, sweep the scanner across the roof of the mouth in a smooth, continuous "S" pattern to fill in the palatal vault.
  4. Buccal: Roll to the buccal and complete the final sweep around the arch.

C — Bite Registration

  1. Have the patient bite down into their natural maximum intercuspation (MIP).
  2. Scan the buccal surfaces of the articulated teeth on both the left and right sides until the software successfully snaps the upper and lower arches together.

Orthodontic-Specific Clinical Tips

  • Distal of terminal molars — the most frequently missed area and critical for aligners. Have the patient close slightly to relax the cheek muscles and shift their jaw toward the side being scanned to create more room for the wand head.
  • Scanning with brackets and wires — metal brackets can cause light scatter. Angle the tip slightly at 45° (gingival-to-occlusal and occlusal-to-gingival) rather than hitting brackets dead-on. "Wiggle" the scanner slightly to capture bracket undercuts.
  • Filling in holes — if the software shows missing data, do not drop the scanner directly onto the void. Start scanning an adjacent well-captured area, let the scanner find its location, then slowly glide over the missing area to fill it in.

Completion & Submission

  1. Review scan for voids, stitching errors, or missing anatomy — use the tips above to fill in as needed.
  2. Approve final scan and label with purpose (e.g., "Treatment records," "Retainer," "Aligner submission").
  3. Export or submit: send to lab, aligner company, or save STL to patient chart as appropriate.
For Invisalign submissions: complete the prescription form in the Invisalign Doctor Site before or immediately after scan submission. Confirm the case is linked to the correct patient account.
📸
Clinical Photography & Radiograph Standards
SOP-RC-003  ·  Clinical Assistant
Standard
▾

Camera — Olympus OMD Settings

⚠️ The camera has been preset to the settings below. Do not change these settings. If anything looks off, restore to the values in this table before shooting.
Mode
A (Aperture)
ISO
200
Flash
Fill In
Metering
Spot
Flash Comp.
0
Lens Focus
0.019 m to ∞
  • F-stop adjustment: Turn the rear command wheel located behind the shoot button.
  • Intraoral photos: Use F22
  • Full face & profile photos: Use F8
📋 A complete records set requires 10 photos (3 extraoral + 7 intraoral) and 2 radiographs (Panoramic + Cephalometric). Retake any photo that does not meet the standards below — substandard records compromise diagnosis and treatment planning.

Extraoral Photos (3)

ShotRequirements
Profile (Lateral)
  • Teeth in occlusion; lips relaxed and in contact
  • Natural head position — eyes looking forward
  • Entire head and neck visible; left eyelash slightly visible
  • Center of frame ~1.0 cm anterior to tragus
  • White or light background — no shadows, no double profile
Frontal Repose
(non-smiling)
  • Patient faces camera; eyes open, looking directly into lens
  • Ears exposed; no distracting eyewear or jewelry
  • Inter-pupillary line horizontal to the frame
  • Center of frame at tip of the nose
  • Crop to face and neck only
Frontal Smiling Same framing and positioning as frontal repose — add a natural social smile (not forced)

Intraoral Photos (7)

ShotRequirements
Frontal Occlusal
  • Teeth in occlusion; occlusal plane horizontal and bisecting the photo
  • Equal display of posterior dentition left and right
Right Lateral
  • Anterior: entire ipsilateral maxillary central incisor visible at minimum
  • Posterior: entire first molars at minimum (extend to 2nd molars if possible)
  • All attached gingiva visible; occlusal plane parallel to frame
Left Lateral Same requirements as right lateral
Right Buccal (Mirror)
  • Taken with mirror; flip horizontally in software to represent patient's right side as a direct view
  • Extend view to second molars if possible
Left Buccal (Mirror) Same requirements as right buccal mirror
Upper Occlusal
  • Mid-palatal raphe centered in frame
  • Frame entire arch; minimize lateral soft tissue display
Lower Occlusal
  • Fill frame with entire mandibular arch through at least the first molars
  • Labial surface of central incisors parallel to bottom of frame; midline centered

Radiographs

TypeRequirements
Cephalometric (Lateral)
  • Full soft tissue profile displayed; occipital region visible
  • Head in natural position — Frankfort Horizontal plane parallel to floor
  • Teeth in occlusion; soft tissue, occiput, and symphysis all visible
Panoramic
  • Correct patient orientation; properly labeled right & left
  • Correct exposure — not over- or under-exposed
  • Both condyles fully visible; symphysis and all anterior teeth in frame
  • Remove all jewelry before exposure

Common Errors — Retake Required If You See These

ErrorCause / Fix
Dark background / double shadow on profileMove patient closer to white background; reposition lighting to eliminate shadow
Patient too distant from cameraMove patient or camera closer; fill the frame properly
Head rotated, not facing camera (frontal)Reposition — patient must look directly into lens with ears exposed
Distracting jewelry or hair over earsAsk patient to remove jewelry and pull hair back before shooting
Photo too darkAdjust lighting or camera exposure settings before retaking
¾ profile instead of true lateralReposition — lateral must be a true 90° side view
Mirror not flipped (buccal)Flip horizontally in software before saving to chart
Ceph: head tilted / not horizontalEnsure Frankfort plane is parallel to floor; reposition patient in cephalostat
Ceph: soft tissue/occiput/symphysis cut offReposition collimator to include full field
Pano: overexposed or underexposedAdjust exposure settings per patient size; refer to machine guide
Pano: R & L labels reversedVerify patient orientation before exposure; correct labeling in software
Pano: condyles or anterior teeth cut offReposition patient — chin on rest, spine straight, biting on bite block
🦷 KöR Whitening System 6 procedures ▾
🔑
KöR Whitening — Overview, Patient Screening & Portal Access
SOP-KOR-001  ·  All Clinical Staff
Reference
▾
SOP ID
SOP-KOR-001
System
KöR Whitening Deep Bleaching™
Portal
korcommunity.com
Login
akhavan.ak@gmail.com
Password
Dmnd2020i
🚫 Contraindications — Do NOT start KöR whitening if: Patient is pregnant or nursing. Whitening during pregnancy or lactation is absolutely contraindicated. Screen at every appointment during active whitening treatment.
🌡️ KöR gel must be refrigerated at all times. Do not leave syringes at room temperature for extended periods and never leave in a hot vehicle. Heat degrades the peroxide and reduces whitening efficacy.
▶ Background — About the KöR System

About the KöR System

KöR Whitening Deep Bleaching™ is considered one of the most effective whitening systems available, capable of treating average discoloration, fluorosis, aging stains, and even severe tetracycline cases. The system works by conditioning teeth to accept and maintain whitening gel for extended periods. The key is the KöR-Seal tray — a custom tray with a tight peripheral seal that isolates gel from saliva contamination, allowing peroxide to remain active for 6–10 hours rather than the 25–35 minutes of conventional trays.

Exam Visit Essentials — Assess Every Patient Before Starting

AssessmentHow to CheckClinical Implication
Translucency Test Hold a light directly behind the incisal edges — translucent edges appear blue/grey Translucent enamel cannot whiten; set expectations carefully. Document with photo.
White Spots (Hypocalcification) Examine teeth under direct light; photograph baseline White spots may appear more pronounced initially, then blend in as surrounding enamel whitens. Inform patient before starting — this is expected and temporary.
Dark / Banding Discoloration Photograph under standard operatory lighting; note location of bands Cervical dark bands (especially tetracycline) may not fully resolve. Manage expectations. Refer to SOP-KOR-005 for tetracycline protocol.
Clencher / Bruxer Assessment Ask about clenching/grinding history; examine for wear facets Bruxers can deform or crack KöR trays. Note on tray Rx so the lab adds a 0.5mm spacer in the tray prescription. Monitor tray integrity at follow-up. See SOP-KOR-006.
Pregnancy / Nursing Screen Ask directly at exam and at every whitening appointment Absolute contraindication. Do not initiate or continue whitening if patient is pregnant or nursing.
Existing Restorations Document crowns, veneers, bonding on teeth to be whitened Restorations will NOT whiten. Patient will need replacement to match new shade if whitening adjacent teeth. Disclose before starting.
Gag Reflex Test with impression tray if concerned Request shorter flanges on tray prescription. Consider KöR-Day protocol (trays only in for 30–60 min, patient awake and comfortable).

Treatment Selection Guide

ProtocolIndicationAt-Home PhaseIn-OfficeSOP
KöR-NightStandard discoloration, most adult patients2 wks overnight (16% carbamide peroxide)NoneKOR-002
KöR-DayCan't wear overnight; sensitive patients2 wks × 30–60 min/day (9% hydrogen peroxide)NoneKOR-002
KöR MaxModerate-to-heavy staining; wants at-home only4–6 wks KöR-Night (stronger gel)NoneKOR-002
KöR UltraBest results for standard cases; dramatic whitening2 wks at-home (KöR-Night)Yes — 3 × 20 minKOR-004
KöR Ultra-TTetracycline staining · Severe fluorosis6–8+ wks at-home (KöR-Night)Yes — 3 × 20 minKOR-005
In-Office Power OnlySingle-visit; patient preferenceNoneYes — 3 × 20 minKOR-003

KöR-Seal Tray Fabrication

  • Take upper and lower digital scans using iTero or Allied Star (AS). Confirm full arch capture with no voids before submitting.
  • Trim style: No scalloping. Trim the tray material in a straight line across the gingival margin — this straight edge is what creates the KöR peripheral seal. Scalloped trays break the seal and allow saliva in.
  • The KöR tray seal allows gel to remain active 6–10 hours vs. 25–35 minutes for conventional trays. This is the cornerstone of the system's effectiveness.
  • Alternative — KöR Lab: Log onto KörCommunity.com to download the lab slip. Send impressions or STL file to KöR Lab for professional tray fabrication.
  • Bruxers: Note "bruxer — add 0.5mm spacer" on the tray prescription to protect tray from occlusal forces.
▶ KöR Community Portal Resources

KöR Community Portal Resources

  • Training videos, clinical protocols, and case studies at korcommunity.com
  • Patient education materials, consent forms, and lab slips
  • Case submission and gel ordering portal
  • Login: akhavan.ak@gmail.com  ·  Password: Dmnd2020i
▶ Product Reference — Gel & Storage Chart

Product Reference

ProductUseStorage
KöR-Night (16% carbamide peroxide)Standard at-home overnight whitening (~8 hrs)Refrigerate
KöR-Day (9% hydrogen peroxide)Daytime at-home whitening (30–60 min)Refrigerate
KöR Max (stronger carbamide peroxide)Extended at-home protocol for resistant stainingRefrigerate
KöR Ultra (34% hydrogen peroxide)In-office whitening treatment — tri-barrel deliveryRefrigerate
KöR Ultra-T (34% HP + boosted formula)In-office — tetracycline & severe stainingRefrigerate
KöR DesensitizerApplied before & after each whitening sessionRoom temp OK
KöR Tri-Barrel Mixer TipIn-office gel delivery — mixes components at point of applicationN/A
🏠
KöR At-Home Whitening Protocol (KöR-Night, KöR-Day, KöR Max)
SOP-KOR-002  ·  Clinical Assistant & Treatment Coordinator
Routine
▾
SOP ID
SOP-KOR-002
KöR-Night
16% carbamide peroxide · worn overnight (~8 hrs)
KöR-Day
9% hydrogen peroxide · worn 30–60 min/day
KöR Max
Higher-concentration carbamide · 4–6 weeks nightly
Standard Duration
KöR-Night / Day: 2 weeks · KöR Max: 4–6 weeks

Tray Delivery Appointment — What to Dispense

  • Custom KöR whitening trays (upper and lower) — verify fit chairside before patient leaves.
  • KöR gel syringes (sufficient supply for the at-home phase — typically 1 syringe per arch per night for 2 weeks).
  • KöR Desensitizer syringes.
  • Written KöR patient instruction sheet (available on KörCommunity portal).
  • Carrying case for trays.

Tray Fit Verification (Chairside)

  • Seat the tray fully — it should extend to but not beyond the gingival margin.
  • No visible gaps between the tray body and tooth surfaces.
  • Patient should not feel significant pain or discomfort on insertion — mild pressure is normal.
  • Check for gum blanching from overtension (trim tray edge if needed).
  • If tray does not seat fully or gaps are visible, retake scan and remake tray before dispensing.

Patient Instructions to Review Chairside (Step by Step)

  1. Apply desensitizer (every night — not just first night): Using the syringe applicator, rub KöR Desensitizer into the outer (facial) surfaces of all teeth with special attention near the gumline. Leave for 1–2 minutes. Spit — do not rinse. This pre-conditions the dentin tubules to reduce sensitivity during whitening.
  2. Load trays: Dispense a small dab of KöR gel (about the size of a small pea) into each tooth reservoir on the inner wall of the tray. Do not overfill — excess gel will contact the gums and cause irritation. Each gel syringe fits one arch for one night.
  3. Seat trays: Press trays firmly against the teeth so the straight tray edges seat completely against the gumline. The peripheral seal must be complete. Wipe away any gel that oozes onto the gums with a clean finger or soft cloth immediately.
  4. Wear time:
    • KöR-Night: Wear while sleeping — approximately 6–10 hours. The tray seal allows gel to stay active the full duration.
    • KöR-Day: Wear 30–60 minutes. Reduce toward 30 minutes if sensitivity occurs.
  5. Remove and rinse: Remove trays. Rinse mouth gently and rinse trays with lukewarm water. Do not use hot water — it can distort the tray material.
  6. Apply desensitizer again after removal to further minimize post-whitening sensitivity.
  7. Store gel in the refrigerator between uses. Store trays in the carrying case provided. Do not leave gel in a hot car or on a countertop for extended periods.
  8. Repeat every night for the full prescribed at-home phase (2 weeks for Night/Day; 4–6 weeks for Max) before returning for the in-office visit if applicable.
⚠️ Sensitivity management: Some sensitivity is normal and expected during the first few nights. If sensitivity is uncomfortable: reduce KöR-Day wear to 30 minutes, or skip one night with KöR-Night and resume the next. Apply desensitizer before and after every session. Persistent severe sensitivity — see SOP-KOR-006 (Super-Sensitive Patient Protocol). Never discontinue without contacting the office.

Dietary Restrictions During Active Treatment

  • Avoid staining foods and beverages for at least 2 hours after removing trays: coffee, tea, red wine, dark sodas, berries, tomato sauce, curry, soy sauce.
  • Do not smoke during the active whitening phase — tobacco significantly reduces whitening efficacy.

At-Home Only Maintenance (After Completing the Initial Phase)

"The Rubber Band Analogy" — Explain to every patient: Think of the teeth like a rubber band being stretched. The whitening process stretches the enamel to a lighter shade. Without regular maintenance, the teeth naturally "bounce back" toward their original color — just like a rubber band returns to its original shape. Monthly maintenance prevents this rebound and keeps teeth permanently white.

Long-Term Maintenance Schedule (At-Home-Only Protocols): After completing the initial 2-week (or 4–6 week) phase, patients wear their trays with KöR-Night gel approximately 1 night per month ongoing. This minimal maintenance is all that is needed to maintain results indefinitely.

🏥
KöR In-Office Power Whitening Protocol
SOP-KOR-003  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Clinical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-KOR-003
Product
KöR Ultra (34% hydrogen peroxide) · tri-barrel syringe
Appt Length
60–90 minutes
Cycles
3 × 20-minute gel applications (60 min total contact)
Indication
In-office power only (no at-home phase) · or as Phase 2 of KOR-004

Setup — Tray and Supplies

  • KöR Ultra tri-barrel syringe (do not mix until immediately before each application — components degrade rapidly once mixed)
  • Light-cure liquid dam / gingival barrier
  • Curing light
  • Cheek retractors
  • Protective eyewear for patient and operator
  • Gauze and high-volume suction
  • KöR Desensitizer
  • Timer

Procedure Steps

  1. Patient preparation: Seat patient; place protective eyewear. Review sensitivity expectations. Confirm no pregnancy or nursing status.
  2. Photograph baseline shade: Document pre-treatment shade with photo against shade guide before any gel contact.
  3. Apply desensitizer: Rub KöR Desensitizer into all facial surfaces of the teeth to be treated for 1–2 minutes. Remove excess with gauze. This step significantly reduces in-office sensitivity.
  4. Insert cheek retractors: Expose all anterior and premolar teeth. Ensure gingival tissue is visible and dry.
  5. Apply gingival barrier: Apply light-cure liquid dam to all exposed gingival tissue — cover completely from gingival margin to approximately 2mm past the mucogingival junction. Light-cure per product instructions. Inspect carefully — any uncovered gum tissue is at risk for chemical burn from the 34% HP gel.
  6. Cycle 1 — Mix and apply KöR Ultra gel: Attach fresh tri-barrel mixer tip and dispense 2–3 strokes to waste (this purges unmixed components). Apply a 1–2mm layer of gel across all tooth facial surfaces in a smooth coat. Do not allow gel to contact gingival barrier margins or soft tissue.
  7. Leave in place exactly 20 minutes. Maintain high-volume suction nearby. Do not leave patient unattended. Monitor comfort — patient should not feel burning; if they do, suction gel immediately and check gingival barrier integrity.
  8. Remove gel — Cycle 1: Thoroughly suction away all gel from tooth surfaces. Wipe with gauze. Do not rinse (water dilutes remaining gel on subsequent cycles).
  9. Repeat for Cycle 2 (attach a new mixer tip for fresh gel). Apply, leave 20 minutes, remove.
  10. Repeat for Cycle 3. Total gel contact time = 60 minutes across 3 cycles.
  11. Remove gingival barrier: After the final cycle, carefully remove the cured liquid dam. Rinse patient thoroughly. Inspect all gingival tissue for any signs of irritation or chemical burn (blanching, redness). Document and treat any irritation.
  12. Apply desensitizer again: Rub KöR Desensitizer into all treated surfaces post-procedure to minimize post-op sensitivity. Patient spits — does not rinse.
  13. Photograph post-treatment shade and compare to baseline.
  14. Provide post-whitening dietary instructions. Patient should avoid staining foods/beverages for 48 hours.
🚫 Never leave KöR Ultra gel on longer than 20 minutes per cycle. Overexposure significantly increases sensitivity and pulpal irritation risk without improving whitening outcome. Always use a timer. Use a fresh mixer tip for every cycle — reusing the tip delivers incompletely mixed, less effective gel.
💡 In-office power only vs. combination: The in-office-only protocol produces good results for mild-to-moderate staining. For more dramatic outcomes, the combination protocol (SOP-KOR-004) — which adds 2 weeks of at-home conditioning before the in-office visit — produces significantly better results, especially for resistant staining.
🔄
KöR Combination Protocol — At-Home + In-Office (KöR Ultra)
SOP-KOR-004  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Clinical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-KOR-004
Phase 1 — At-Home
2 weeks nightly · KöR-Night (16% carbamide peroxide)
Phase 2 — In-Office
KöR Ultra · 3 × 20-min cycles (see SOP-KOR-003)
Maintenance
Accelerated schedule → long-term 1 night/month
Total Appts
3 (Scan/Consult · Tray delivery + instructions · In-office treatment)
▶ Background — Why the Combination Protocol Works Best

Why the Combination Protocol Works Best

The 2-week at-home conditioning phase saturates the enamel and dentin with oxidizing agents, reducing the natural buffering capacity of the tooth. This "conditioning" allows the in-office KöR Ultra gel to penetrate far more deeply on the day of treatment, producing dramatically whiter results than in-office treatment alone. It also significantly reduces sensitivity during the in-office visit because the pulpal tissue has adapted to peroxide over the preceding two weeks.

Protocol Sequence

ApptWhat HappensTimeline
1Patient screening (SOP-KOR-001) · iTero or Allied Star (AS) scan · Submit tray orderConsult/Exam
2Deliver KöR-Night gel + desensitizer + custom trays · Verify tray fit · Review at-home instructions (SOP-KOR-002)1–2 wks after Appt 1
At HomePatient wears KöR-Night trays every night for 2 full weeks · Uses desensitizer before and after each session2 weeks
3In-office KöR Ultra treatment — 3 × 20-min cycles (full SOP-KOR-003 procedure)Immediately after at-home phase
MaintenanceAccelerated maintenance schedule → then 1 night/month ongoing (see below)Ongoing after Appt 3

Accelerated Maintenance Schedule (Immediately After In-Office Appt)

After completing the in-office session, patients follow this accelerated "tapering" schedule before transitioning to long-term maintenance. This schedule consolidates and locks in the whitening result:

Week(s)Frequency
Weeks 1–2 post in-office3 nights per week with KöR-Night gel
Weeks 3–42 nights per week
Weeks 5–61 night per week
Weeks 7–8Every other week (1 night)
Long-term (month 3+)1 night per month — ongoing indefinitely
💡 Rubber Band Analogy for Patient Education: "Think of your teeth like a rubber band being stretched. The whitening process stretches them to a lighter shade. Without regular maintenance, they naturally 'bounce back' — just like a rubber band. One night per month keeps the stretch permanent." Use this analogy at every post-treatment appointment to emphasize compliance with maintenance.
⚠️ Maintenance is non-negotiable. Without at least 1 night per month, gradual re-darkening will occur over 12–24 months regardless of initial outcome. Document patient education regarding maintenance in the chart. Provide refill gel at each recall appointment.
⚗️
KöR Tetracycline Deep Bleaching Protocol (KöR Ultra-T)
SOP-KOR-005  ·  Orthodontist & Clinical Assistant
Advanced
▾
SOP ID
SOP-KOR-005
Indication
Tetracycline staining · Severe intrinsic discoloration · Severe fluorosis
At-Home Phase
6–8 weeks minimum (KöR-Night nightly) — may extend to 6 months
In-Office
KöR Ultra-T (boosted 34% HP formula) · 3 × 20-min cycles
Dr. Approval
Required — Dr. Akhavan must assess and approve case before starting
⚠️ Dr. Akhavan must evaluate every tetracycline case before initiating treatment. Severity grading determines at-home phase duration (6 weeks to 6 months). Set realistic expectations with the patient before starting — results require significant time and strict compliance, and some banding may persist. This is not a treatment failure.
▶ Reference — Tetracycline Severity Grading

Tetracycline Severity Grading (KöR Classification)

GradeAppearanceExpected At-Home PhaseExpected Outcome
Grade 1Light yellow or light grey — uniform staining, minimal banding6–8 weeksExcellent — near-total resolution
Grade 2Moderate yellow-grey or grey — some banding visible8–12 weeksVery good — significant lightening, mild residual banding possible
Grade 3Dark grey or blue-grey — prominent horizontal banding3–6 monthsGood — substantial improvement, some banding will persist
Grade 4Very dark, widespread banding, possible blue/black bandsUp to 6 months+Variable — partial improvement; may need veneers post-whitening

Protocol Sequence

StepWhatTimeline
1Case evaluation by Dr. Akhavan — grade severity, photograph, set expectations, confirm no contraindicationsConsult
2iTero or Allied Star (AS) scan → fabricate KöR trays → deliver with KöR-Night gel + desensitizer · Review SOP-KOR-002 instructionsAppt 1
3At-home phase: KöR-Night every night for 6–8 weeks minimum (Grade 3–4: extend as directed by Dr. Akhavan). Patient applies desensitizer before and after every session.At home
4Progress evaluation — compare to baseline photographs; confirm adequate lightening before scheduling in-office session. Refill gel as needed.Mid-treatment check
5In-office KöR Ultra-T treatment — 3 × 20-min cycles following SOP-KOR-003 procedure (note: Ultra-T, not Ultra)Appt 2
6Continue at-home KöR-Night for additional 2–4 weeks post in-office if indicated by Dr. AkhavanAt home
7Accelerated maintenance schedule (3/wk → 2/wk → 1/wk → every other week → 1–2 nights/month ongoing)Ongoing

Patient Counseling — What to Tell Every Tetracycline Patient

  • Tetracycline staining is the most challenging type to treat — results require patience, time, and perfect compliance with nightly wear.
  • Banding patterns (horizontal stripes) may persist even after significant lightening — this is expected and is not a failure. The bands lighten progressively but the darkest bands (especially cervical) may never fully disappear.
  • Most tetracycline patients achieve 6–16 shades of improvement depending on severity grade. Document expectations in writing before starting.
  • Sensitivity is common, especially in the first 1–2 weeks. Apply desensitizer every single night. Reduce wear time if needed (see SOP-KOR-006 for super-sensitive protocol).
  • Skipping nights significantly reduces the total outcome. Every night matters.
  • Maintenance after completion is critical — 1–2 nights per month prevents rebound darkening.
📋 Documentation: Photograph baseline shade, banding pattern, and tooth surfaces before starting. Repeat photographs at every progress appointment. Final photographs post-in-office and at 3-month maintenance check. Document all patient education and expectation conversations in the chart.
🔧
KöR Troubleshooting — Sensitivity, Poor Results & Tray Issues
SOP-KOR-006  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Reference
▾
SOP ID
SOP-KOR-006
Purpose
Clinical decision support for common KöR whitening problems

Problem 1 — Super-Sensitive Patient (7-Step Protocol)

If a patient reports significant sensitivity that is interfering with compliance, follow this escalating management protocol in order:

  1. Extend desensitizer pre-application time to 10–15 minutes before seating trays (instead of 1–2 minutes). Allow it to fully penetrate the dentin tubules before any peroxide contact.
  2. Reduce wear time on first night(s): Start with only 1 hour the first night. Add 30–60 minutes each subsequent night until reaching the target wear time (overnight for KöR-Night; 30–60 min for KöR-Day).
  3. Alternate nights: On nights when whitening gel is not worn, apply desensitizer only — no gel. This allows recovery while continuing to condition the teeth.
  4. Apply desensitizer immediately after removing trays (in addition to before). Double application significantly reduces post-whitening sensitivity.
  5. Prescribe or recommend OTC fluoride toothpaste or sensitivity toothpaste (e.g., Sensodyne) for use throughout the whitening period. Patient uses this toothpaste normally throughout the day.
  6. If sensitivity remains uncomfortable after steps 1–5: Switch from KöR-Night (16% carbamide) to KöR-Day (9% hydrogen peroxide). Lower concentration with shorter wear time significantly reduces sensitivity while still producing results.
  7. If sensitivity is still unmanageable: Temporarily pause whitening for 3–5 days. Resume with 30-minute KöR-Day sessions. Consult Dr. Akhavan if patient cannot tolerate any whitening.
💡 Sensitivity during KöR whitening is typically temporary and resolves within 24–48 hours of removing the tray. True pulpal pathology is extremely rare but should be considered if sensitivity persists beyond 48 hours or is severe and spontaneous.

Problem 2 — Disappointing At-Home Whitening Results

If a patient returns after the at-home phase with minimal or no visible whitening, systematically evaluate the following:

CheckQuestion to AskFix
Tray Fit Is the tray fully seating and sealing against the gumline? Check chairside (see tray fit checklist below). Remake tray if gaps are present.
Compliance Was the patient wearing the tray every night for the prescribed duration? Review compliance without judgment. Extend the at-home phase by 1–2 additional weeks.
Gel Storage Was the gel refrigerated? Was it ever left in a hot car or on a countertop for extended periods? Replace gel supply if heat exposure is suspected. Reinforce refrigeration instructions.
Gel Loading Was patient applying enough gel to each reservoir? Too little gel = minimal contact with tooth surface. Demonstrate correct loading technique chairside. Each reservoir should be about 1/3 full.
Staining Source Heavy smoker? Daily coffee/tea/red wine? Ongoing staining intake counteracts whitening. Counsel on dietary restrictions during treatment.
Protocol Match Is KöR-Night sufficient for the degree of staining? Does the patient need KöR Max or a combination protocol? Consult Dr. Akhavan — may need to upgrade to KöR Max (stronger gel, longer phase) or add in-office session (SOP-KOR-004).

Problem 3 — Tray Fit Issues (Checklist)

Perform this check at the tray delivery appointment and at any follow-up where fit is questioned:

  • ✅ Tray seats fully — all posterior teeth engaged, not riding up anteriorly.
  • ✅ Tray edges extend to but do not overlap the gingival margin — no significant gum coverage.
  • ✅ No visible gap between tray body and labial/buccal surfaces of teeth.
  • ✅ No blanching of gingival tissue from overtension (if blanching: trim tray edge slightly).
  • ✅ Mild resistance on removal is expected — tray should not fall out loosely.
  • ✅ Patient feels no severe pain on insertion — mild pressure is normal.
  • ❌ If gaps are visible → tray does not have adequate seal → retake scan and remake tray.
  • ❌ If tray does not seat on one or more teeth → block-out resin may be needed or scan retaken.

Problem 4 — Bruxer / Clencher Damaging Trays

  • KöR trays worn by bruxers can develop holes, cracks, or deformation at occlusal contact points.
  • If tray is damaged: patient must stop wearing the damaged tray (compromised seal = reduced efficacy and possible irritation from gel leak).
  • Retake scan and remake tray — specify "bruxer — add 0.5mm occlusal spacer" on the tray Rx.
  • The occlusal spacer reduces force transmission through the tray during clenching and extends tray life.
  • Advise patient to wear a night guard after completing whitening to protect the whitening trays during maintenance.
🗂️ Front Office 7 procedures ▾
📅
Appointment Scheduling & Confirmation
SOP-FO-001  ·  Front Office Coordinator
Admin
▾

Scheduling Guidelines

New Patient Exam
60 min
Records Appt
30–45 min
Full Bonding
60–90 min
Adjustment
20–30 min
Debond
60–90 min
Emergency
15–20 min

Confirmation Protocol

  • Send automated reminder 48 hours before appointment (text + email).
  • If no confirmation received, call 24 hours prior.
  • Document confirmation status in scheduling software.

No-Show / Late Cancel Policy

  • Document no-show in patient chart with date and time.
  • Call patient same day to reschedule.
  • Three no-shows without contact: escalate to treatment coordinator to review care plan.
💳
Insurance Verification & Billing
SOP-FO-002  ·  Treatment Coordinator
Admin
▾

Before New Patient Exam

  1. Collect insurance card (front and back) and verify benefits online or by phone.
  2. Confirm: lifetime orthodontic maximum, age limits, coverage percentage, deductible, and waiting period.
  3. Note any pre-authorization/predetermination requirements.

At Treatment Start

  1. Submit predetermination or prior authorization if required (include X-rays, photos, narrative).
  2. File initial claim with banding date, diagnosis code, and full treatment fee.
  3. Set up payment plan for patient responsibility; collect initial down payment.

Ongoing Billing

  • Submit progress claims on the frequency specified by the insurance carrier (typically quarterly or monthly).
  • Post EOBs (Explanation of Benefits) and reconcile with patient ledger within 5 business days of receipt.
  • Follow up on unpaid claims after 30 days.
📊
AR Aging & Collections Protocol
SOP-FO-003  ·  Financial Coordinator / Treatment Coordinator
Admin
▾

Regular Monitoring

  1. Run AR Aging Reports regularly.
  2. Follow up with phone calls, texts, and email.
  3. Focus on 1–30 days past due — highest probability of successful collection.

Early Intervention

  1. Make an Alternative Arrangement (AA) as soon as financial difficulty is sensed.
  2. Work with the responsible party to develop a realistic payment plan.
  3. Treatment does not progress on patients with a past-due balance that has no plan for resolution.

If AA Is Broken

  1. Change the appointment type to Deb / Finish.
  2. Send both a certified letter and a regular postal letter to the responsible party.
  3. Schedule one appointment for a Re-tie / Hygiene Check.
  4. If no AA is made at or before that appointment, the next visit may be a Debond.

Discontinuation of Treatment

If a major hardship (e.g., job loss) makes it impossible to continue, discuss the possibility of D/C treatment with Dr. Akhavan before taking any action.

⚠ Only the Financial Coordinator (FC) can change a patient's status to Inactive Financial. Do not change this status without FC authorization.
🗓️

412Deliver Habit Appliance
413Deliver Halterman
414Deliver Hawley with Finger Spring
415Deliver Herbst
416Deliver Horseshoe Appliance
417Deliver Lower Lingual Holding Arch (LLHA)
418Deliver MARA
419Deliver Mouthguard
420Deliver Nance
421Deliver Nightguard (Custom)
422Deliver Pontic, NAW
423Deliver Quad Helix
424Deliver Reset Retainer
425Deliver Retainer, Essix
426Deliver Retainer, Essix with Pontic
427Deliver Retainer, Essix — Smile Assurance
428Deliver Retainer, Essix + Fixed Upper/Lower Retainer
429Deliver Retainer, Essix + Fixed Upper/Lower Retainer — Smile Assurance
432Deliver Retainer, Essix + Fixed Upper and Lower Retainer
433Deliver Retainer, Essix + Fixed Upper and Lower Retainer — Smile Assurance
434Deliver Retainer, Hawley
435Deliver Retainer, Hawley with Pontic
436Deliver Scallops and Essix
437Deliver Schwartz
438Deliver Splint
439Deliver TPA
440Deliver Williams

Checks

CodeDescription
501Retention Check — First Year (No Charge)
502Retention Check — After First Year, With Fee

504Check / Activate Appliance

506OH Check

Procedures

CodeDescription
510Laser Recontouring
511Soft Tissue Exposure with Laser
512Frenectomy
513Deliver TAD(s) 1–4
514Enameloplasty

Wires / Adjustments

CodeDescription
600NAW (1–2) Simple
601NAW (1–2) Complex
602NAW (1–2) Complex, IPR

604NAW (1–2) with Multiple Pontics
605NAW (1–2) Detail
606Transfer In Patient NAW (1–2) Complex
607NAW (1–2) INBRACE Complex
608SAW (1–2) Simple
609SAW (1–2) Complex
610SAW (1–2) Detail
611SAW (1–2) Detail, Check to Finish, Finishing Elastics
