Patient Statuses

StatusMeaning
Start NeededExam complete, X-rays/photos taken, Tx recommended, not yet scheduled
Start ScheduledStart appointment actually scheduled
Growth GuidanceExam complete — not yet ready for treatment; in recall or observation
Phase II PendingBetween Phase I and Phase II
Consult ScheduledExam and records complete, consultation scheduled
Specialist / DDSReferred to specialist or GP
Maintenance (Financial)On tx hold for financial reasons — limit 60 days
Active — Comp BracesCAT
Active — Comp InvisalignINV
Active — Comp Next LevelIHA
Active — InterceptiveLT
Active — Phase I BracesPHI
Active — Phase I InvisalignPHINV
Active — Phase II BracesPHIIHA
Active — RetreatRETX
RetentionRCK2 — post-treatment retention phase
InactiveNo tx needed / declined
📋
Financial Presentation & Treatment Consent
SOP-FO-005  ·  Treatment Coordinator
Front Office
▾
The financial presentation is a conversation, not a transaction. The goal is to make the investment feel clear, fair, and achievable — so the family says yes with confidence. Use the language from SOP-FO-005 (Do Say / Don't Say) throughout.

Before Entering the Consultation Room

  • Confirm treatment plan is entered in Edge by Dr. Akhavan.
  • Pull the patient's insurance information and verify benefits if applicable.
  • Prepare the financial options worksheet with the fee, insurance estimate, and payment plan options.
  • Have all consent and agreement forms ready to sign.

Presentation Flow

  1. Build rapport — brief friendly conversation before discussing treatment. Reference something from the patient's chart or what Dr. Akhavan mentioned.
  2. Review the treatment plan — explain what treatment involves in plain language (appliance type, estimated duration, visits). Do not use clinical jargon without explaining it.
  3. Present the fee — state the total fee clearly: "Your fee for treatment is ____." Do not say "dollars." Do not say "cost."
  4. Present insurance — if applicable: "Your insurance will cover approximately ____, which brings your out-of-pocket to ____." Clarify this is an estimate.
  5. Present payment options — offer the down payment + monthly arrangement: "We offer payment arrangements starting at ____ down and ____ per month."
  6. Ask for the decision — "Would you like to get started today?" Do not say "How do you plan to pay?"

If the Family Needs Time

  • That is completely fine — do not pressure. Offer to email them a written summary of the treatment plan and fee options.
  • Set a specific follow-up: "I'll reach out in a couple of days — does that work?"
  • Log the outcome in Edge under the patient file.

Documents to Sign at Start

  • Orthodontic treatment agreement (financial contract)
  • HIPAA acknowledgment
  • Patient / guardian consent for treatment
  • Photo and records release (if applicable)
🚨 Do not schedule the bonding appointment until ALL documents are signed and scanned into the patient chart. No exceptions — a minor patient requires a parent or legal guardian signature.

After the Presentation

  • Enter the payment arrangement into Edge and confirm the down payment is collected or scheduled.
  • Schedule the bonding appointment (confirm chair time with clinical team).
  • Give the patient/family a written summary of what to expect at their next visit.
  • Log outcome (started, pending, declined) in Edge notes.
🌅
Opening & End-of-Day Protocols
SOP-FO-006  ·  Clinical Assistant & Front Office
Daily
▾

Morning Opening — Clinical

  1. Power on all equipment: Sensimatic 700SE, panoramic unit, iTero scanner, curing lights, suction.
  2. Run a spore test (biological indicator) in the M11 autoclave if it is a scheduled spore test day. Log result per SOP-EQ-002.
  3. Check sterilization pouches from the previous day — confirm all indicators changed and pouches are intact before placing in service.
  4. Stock each operatory: barriers applied to all contact surfaces, gloves in correct sizes, suction tips, cups, gauze, and standard tray supplies.
  5. Pull first patient's chart; confirm records are complete and any appliances/retainers are ready.
  6. Confirm iTero is synced and camera is charged and set to correct settings (SOP-RC-003).

Morning Opening — Front Office

  1. Open Edge and review the full day's schedule — flag any patients with outstanding balances, missing forms, or pending consents.
  2. Confirm any same-day lab work or retainers are printed and ready.
  3. Check the Asana retainer fabrication board — confirm any retainers due today are completed and bagged.
  4. Check messages (Weave / voicemail) from after-hours and triage before the first patient arrives.

End-of-Day — Clinical

  1. Complete full operatory turnover on all treatment rooms per SOP-IC-003.
  2. Run the M11 autoclave for any remaining instruments; log the cycle (load #, cycle type, result) in the sterilization log.
  3. Power down: suction, curing lights, panoramic unit. Leave iTero and computer on if needed for next-day sync.
  4. Restock any supplies that are low — flag for ordering if below par level.
  5. Wipe down the Sensimatic 700SE exterior; confirm handpiece is sterilized and stored per SOP-EQ-001.
  6. Confirm all instruments are sterilized, pouched, and stored — nothing left unpackaged overnight.

End-of-Day — Front Office

  1. Reconcile the day's payments in Edge — confirm all charges and payments are posted.
  2. Send any outstanding patient communications (appointment reminders, follow-up messages).
  3. Review tomorrow's schedule: confirm all charts are complete, any needed records or appliances are queued.
  4. Add any new retainer cases to Asana with the correct due date (2 business days from scan).
  5. Lock the front desk and secure the office per practice closing checklist.
🧹
Down Time & Week Off Tasks
SOP-FO-007  ·  All Clinical Staff
Admin
▾
SOP ID
SOP-FO-007
Staff
All Clinical Staff
When
During slow periods, between patients, or on days off

Post this list in a communal area of the office. These tasks keep the practice running smoothly and should be completed proactively whenever there is downtime.

Preparation Tasks

  • Make take-home bags — Aligners, RPE/Schwartz, Initial Bonding
  • Make debonding gift bags — Children & Adults
  • Fill color o-ties in all drawers

Restocking

  • Restock drawers with supplies: suctions, cotton rolls, elastics, scanning tips, etc.
  • Stock the impression area: alginate, upper and lower trays, alginate flavoring, lab slips, bowl cleaner
  • Put away and check off any incoming supply orders

Equipment & Maintenance

  • Deep clean chairs: bases, cushions, lights, and light bar
  • Run suctions
  • Shock water lines and refill water bottles
  • Run sterile and make trays
  • Deep clean cupboards

Patient Care

  • See comfort /"emergency/" patients as needed

Common Areas

  • Clean and wipe down lobby area
  • Deep clean brushing sink
  • Deep clean bathroom
  • Tidy lobby — recycle old magazines if applicable
⚙️ Equipment 2 procedures ▾
⚡
Sensimatic 700SE Electrosurge — Use & Maintenance
SOP-EQ-001  ·  Clinical Assistant & Orthodontist
Equipment Safety
▾
🚨 DO NOT open the case — no user-serviceable parts inside. Always turn the unit OFF before touching or changing electrodes. Keep your foot off the pedal during electrode changes.

Contraindications

Do not use on patients with cardiac pacemakers, internal defibrillators, intracorporeal fluid pumps, or any implantable electronic device. Consult a physician before use if patient is pregnant or has any condition that may be affected by high-frequency current.

Setup — Connecting the Unit

  1. Confirm the AC rocker switch on the rear of the unit is OFF before plugging in.
  2. Plug the indifferent (patient) plate cable and the handpiece cable into the matching color-coded sockets on the front panel.
  3. Place the indifferent plate firmly against the patient's upper back — always with a thin layer of clothing between the plate and bare skin. Do not attach to the metal frame of the chair.
  4. Turn the unit OFF and select the correct electrode for the procedure. Insert the electrode fully into the handpiece — no metal shaft should be exposed. Lock the collar.
  5. Flip the rear AC switch to ON. Rotate the mode knob from OFF to the desired waveform. Set the power level. Verify indicator LEDs are lit.

Operating Modes & Power Settings

ModeKnob PositionBest ForStarting Power
CUTMode 1Clean incisions to be sutured (least coagulation)4 (needle) / 5 (loop)
CUT/COAGMode 2 ✦ Most commonCutting with bleeding control — most dentistry procedures4–6 depending on electrode
COAGMode 3Hemostasis only, no cutting (ball electrode)3
Ball electrode (C3) coagulation
Start at 3
Needle incision
Start at 4
Small loop excision
Start at 5
Larger loop excision
Start at 6
Fulguration / carbonization
Mode 1, Start at 8

Electrode Quick-Reference

ElectrodeTypeOrthodontic & Common Uses
T2Scalpel PointExposing teeth for orthodontic eruption, frenectomy, periodontal flaps, pericoronitis
T5Large Tissue Shaving LoopCementing restorations, edentulous ridge recontour, hyperplastic tissue removal
T8Vertical LoopExposing teeth for eruption, crown lengthening, esthetic contouring, tissue biopsy
T16Horizontal LoopAccess to subgingival caries, crown lengthening, esthetic contouring
C3Hemostasis BallBleeding control & coagulation for a cleaner operative field
AP1.5Troughing PointWidening gingival sulcus for impression prep margin

Technique Tips

  • Always use the lowest effective power setting — less power means less trauma and faster healing.
  • Tissue should be slightly moist but not wet. Dry tissue causes dragging; excess moisture reduces effectiveness.
  • Keep the electrode in constant, smooth, uninterrupted motion. Never linger in one spot — allow ~10 seconds between cuts for tissue to cool.
  • Use only non-conducting (plastic) instruments — mirror, retractor, saliva evacuation tube — during the procedure.
  • Activate by pressing the foot pedal; the green RF indicator light and audio tone will confirm the unit is active.
  • Periodically wipe carbonized tissue tags from the electrode with an alcohol-moistened pad (de-energize first by lifting foot from pedal).

Odor Control

Electrosurgery generates tissue fumes. Use high-volume oral evacuation throughout the procedure. Air freshener prior to surgery and a small dab of mentholated ointment (Vicks VapoRub) on the patient's upper lip also help minimize odor.

Cleaning & Sterilization

  1. Rinse the handpiece and electrodes under warm running water for 30 seconds to remove external soil and debris.
  2. Scrub with a soft soapy brush (non-ammoniated detergent or dishwashing soap only — no ammoniated cleansers or disinfectants).
  3. Rinse again for 30 seconds; blot dry with a lint-free towel.
  4. Wipe the unit body with a lint-free towel saturated in an EPA-approved, hospital-grade intermediate or high-level disinfectant (minimum 2.5% Glutaraldehyde solution). Follow the disinfectant manufacturer's instructions.
  5. Dry thoroughly.
  6. Autoclave the handpiece and electrodes using the M11 Pouches cycle (132°C / 270°F, 5 min) if packaged in a sterilization pouch. Allow a full 15-minute cool-down before removing. See SOP-EQ-002 for complete M11 cycle reference.
⚠ DO NOT use dry heat or Chemclave on the handpiece or electrodes. Do not immerse the main unit in water. Inspect the sterilization pouch for integrity before use — discard and reorder if in doubt.

Electrode Maintenance

  • Inspect the plastic insulation sheath on every electrode before each use — replace if cracked or damaged.
  • Do not bend the metal shaft where it is insulated.
  • Wipe electrodes clean between uses with an alcohol-moistened pad to remove charred tissue. Dirty electrodes cause unnecessary tissue damage.
  • Electrodes are consumable items and should be periodically replaced — they are not covered by warranty.

Troubleshooting

ProblemLikely Cause(s)
Electrode drags, not cutting cleanlyDirty electrode; tissue too dry; penetration too deep (>2mm); power too low; indifferent plate not in full contact
Excessive tissue removal or thinningWrong electrode selected; power too high; poor technique (slow or erratic motion)
Retarded healing / sloughingPower too high; staying too long in one spot; electrode movement too slow; tissue too moist or too dry
Hemostasis not achievedIncrease power one step at a time until coagulation is visible (whitish blanching)
📞 Parkell Technical Support: 1-800-243-7446 (M–F, 8:30 AM – 5:00 PM EST)  ·  techsupport@parkell.com
🫧
Midmark M11 Steam Sterilizer — Use & Maintenance
SOP-EQ-002  ·  Clinical Assistant
Equipment Infection Control
▾
⚠ Use distilled or demineralized water only. Tap water contains minerals and chlorides that will corrode the chamber and void the warranty.

Before Each Use — Daily Setup

  1. Check the water level indicator on the front of the unit.
  2. If low, open the door and pour distilled water into the fill opening until it reaches the top of the fill level label on the water level indicator tube.
  3. Inspect door gasket and trays — remove any visible debris or residue.
  4. Confirm the unit powers on and displays no error codes before loading instruments.

Loading Instruments

  • Place instruments in single-layer arrangement — do not overload trays or stack pouches flat on top of each other.
  • Stand pouches on edge (vertically) in the rack so steam can fully circulate around all surfaces.
  • Hinged instruments (e.g., scissors, forceps) must be left in the open position for steam to reach all surfaces.
  • Confirm pouch seal integrity before loading — reseal or replace any compromised pouches.

Sterilization Cycles

CycleTempSterilize TimeUse For
Unwrapped132°C (270°F)3 minUnpackaged instruments for immediate use
Pouches132°C (270°F)5 minSingle-wrapped pouched instruments
Packs121°C (250°F)30 minLarger wrapped packs, cloth, rubber items
Handpieces132°C (270°F)6 minFiber-optic and turbine handpieces

Total cycle time includes heat-up, sterilization, and drying. Do not open the door until the cycle is fully complete and the display shows the cycle is finished.

After the Cycle

  1. Allow the chamber to fully depressurize before opening the door.
  2. Open door slightly and allow a 15-minute cool-down before removing pouches — removing hot pouches can cause condensation and compromise the sterile barrier.
  3. Inspect each pouch: verify the chemical indicator has changed color, the seal is intact, and the pouch is completely dry. Any wet pack is considered non-sterile — reprocess.
  4. Document the cycle in the sterilization log: date, cycle type, operator initials, chemical indicator result, and load contents.
  5. Store sterile packs in a clean, dry, closed cabinet — away from moisture, heat, or traffic areas.

Weekly Maintenance

The unit will automatically display "PERFORM WEEKLY MAINTENANCE" every 7 days.

  1. Drain the reservoir completely using the drain tube on the front of the unit into an appropriate container.
  2. Remove trays, the rack, and the bottom plate from the chamber.
  3. Wash removed items and the inside of the chamber with mild soap or Midmark Speed-Clean and water. Use a soft cloth — no abrasives.
  4. Rinse all components thoroughly with distilled water and dry completely.
  5. Reassemble and refill the reservoir with fresh distilled water.
  6. Run an empty cycle to flush the system before returning to clinical use.

Monthly Maintenance

  1. Deep-clean the chamber: Apply Midmark Speed-Clean (or equivalent autoclave chamber cleaner) to the interior walls, floor, and trays. Allow to dwell per product instructions, then scrub gently with a soft nylon brush. Rinse thoroughly with distilled water and dry.
  2. Inspect and clean the door gasket: Remove the gasket from the door groove. Clean the gasket and groove with a damp cloth — remove all residue, scale, and debris. Inspect the gasket for cracks, tears, hardening, or flattening. Replace immediately if any damage is found — a compromised gasket will cause cycle failures and pressure leaks.
  3. Inspect trays and rack: Check all stainless trays and the wire rack for rust spots, corrosion, or warping. Discard and replace any corroded trays — rust contamination can compromise sterility and damage the chamber.
  4. Check the drain tube and reservoir: Inspect the drain tube for kinks, cracks, or buildup. Flush with distilled water. Wipe out the reservoir with a clean soft cloth to remove any sediment.
  5. Inspect the door latch and hinge: Verify the door closes and seals smoothly with no resistance. The latch should engage cleanly — report any stiffness or misalignment to Dr. Akhavan for service evaluation.
  6. Run a complete empty cycle after reassembly before returning to clinical use. Confirm no error codes are displayed and the cycle completes normally.
  7. Document in the maintenance log: Record date, tasks completed, gasket condition, and operator initials.
⚠ Do not use bleach, iodine-based cleaners, or abrasive pads inside the chamber — these will corrode stainless steel and void the warranty. Use only Midmark Speed-Clean or a Midmark-approved chamber cleaner.

Biological Indicator (Spore) Testing

  • Run a spore test (Biological Indicator) at least weekly — or per your state dental board requirements.
  • Place the BI in the center of a full load, run the appropriate cycle, and mail or incubate per the BI manufacturer's instructions.
  • Use a Chemical Indicator (CI) in every load — this does not replace BI testing, but confirms basic sterilization conditions were met.
  • If a BI returns a positive (failed) result: immediately stop using the sterilizer, quarantine all loads processed since the last passing test, notify Dr. Akhavan, and contact Midmark service.
  • Document all BI results in the sterilization log.

Troubleshooting

IssueLikely Cause / Action
Wet packs at end of cycleOverloading; pouches laid flat; door opened too early — allow full cool-down before removing
Unit won't reach temperatureLow water level; scale buildup; contact Midmark service
Door won't seal / cycle won't startDoor gasket damaged or debris on sealing surface — inspect and clean gasket
"Low Water" errorRefill reservoir with distilled water only
Chemical indicator didn't changeWrong cycle selected; overloading; reprocess and investigate
🔧 Midmark recommends verification by an authorized service provider every 10 years or 10,000 cycles (whichever comes first), with annual inspections thereafter.  ·  Midmark Support: 1-800-MIDMARK (1-800-643-6275)
🔬 Lab 7 procedures ▾
🧪
KöR-Seal Tray Fabrication — Lab Protocol (Ministar S)
SOP-KOR-007  ·  Lab Technician / Clinical Assistant
Lab
▾
SOP ID
SOP-KOR-007
Thermoformer
Ministar S (pressure machine) — flat platform
Scan Source
iTero or Allied Star (AS) — digital workflow, no stone
Tray Material
KöR EVA 0.040" (1.0 mm) soft thermoform sheet (round for Ministar S)
Reference Video
korcommunity.com → "KöR Whitening Tray Fabrication" training video
ℹ️ NLO Workflow Note: We use digital scans (iTero or Allied Star) and 3D printed/milled models — not PVS impressions or stone. Steps referencing impression trimming and stone pouring from the KöR reference manual do not apply. Start at Step 1 below with your printed/milled model. If you prefer to outsource tray fabrication, log into KörCommunity.com to download the lab slip and mail models to the KöR Lab.
⚠️ Do not use the Ministar S pellet/bead tray as the platform. Always use the flat platform. No paper cut-out is needed with a pressure machine — that step only applies to vacuum-forming machines.

Supplies Needed

ItemNotes
3D printed or milled arch model (from iTero / AS scan)Must be fully dry before proceeding
KöR Light-Cure Block-Out ResinFor reservoirs and model block-out
Curing light + protective eyewearRequired for curing block-out resin
99% isopropyl alcohol + 2×2 gauzeFor wiping reservoir surfaces to create rough texture
Handler SLICK-88 / SPL-88 Silicone/Teflon SprayModel separator — not Pam or oil-based sprays
Ministar S thermoformerUse flat platform only
KöR round EVA sheets (0.040" / 1.0 mm, soft)Round sheets for Ministar S — order from KöR
KöR Scissors (sharp, non-serrated)For trimming — no serrated scissors, no hot knife, no flame
KöR Model Definer or sharp Hollenback carverFor lightly defining gingival margins if needed
Damp paper towel / small water bathFor cooling tray after thermoforming

Step 1 — Prepare the Printed/Milled Model

ℹ️ NLO Digital Workflow: We do not use a model trimmer. The model base and lingual/palatal dimensions are set digitally in PreForm before printing. Set the base to approximately 6.4 mm (¼ inch) thickness and trim the lingual/palatal area to within ~6.4 mm (¼ inch) of the teeth in the PreForm support/base settings before sending to the printer.
  1. Inspect the printed model for any support artifact bumps, print-layer artifacts, or voids. The gingival margins must be clean and sharp. If any support nubs remain on tooth surfaces, carefully remove them with a scalpel — do not apply pressure to gingival margin areas.
  2. Lightly define gingival margins only if any margin is not sharply defined: Using the KöR Model Definer (or a sharp-tip Hollenback carver), lightly "flick off" any blebs. Lightly define the facial gingival crevice only where needed. Do NOT score ("dig into") the margins — only lightly define. With a well-calibrated printer and good scan, defining is rarely needed.
  3. The model must be completely dry (room temperature, fully cured) before proceeding to reservoir placement. Freshly printed resin models must be post-cured per the printer's protocol before use.

Step 2 — Place Reservoirs (KöR Light-Cure Block-Out Resin)

🚫 Do NOT use dense, smooth-surface block-out resin without the isopropyl alcohol treatment below. A smooth resin surface impedes airflow during thermoforming and prevents the tray from forming precisely into the critical gingival margin and crevice area (the 1–1.5mm KöR-Seal zone). The isopropyl alcohol wipe is mandatory.
  1. Confirm the model is fully dry before applying reservoirs.
  2. Apply a small amount of KöR Light-Cure Block-Out Resin to the facial surface of each tooth to be whitened. Use the side of the dispensing tip to spread the resin into an even, smooth layer.

    3. KöR Light-Cure Block-Out Resin applied to facial surfaces — note the 0.5–1 mm thickness and 1–1.5 mm clearance from gingival margin.

  3. Reservoir thickness: 0.5–1mm only. Do not overfill — you may even see small areas of the model surface showing through the reservoir prior to curing. Strive for a smooth, even surface.
  4. Reservoir placement boundaries:
    • Place reservoirs on all teeth first molar to first molar only. Do NOT place reservoirs on second molars — the tray must grip the second molars tightly on both facial and lingual surfaces for retention.
    • Keep reservoirs 1.0–1.5mm away from the gingival margin and from all interproximal contacts.
    • Do NOT extend reservoirs onto incisal or occlusal surfaces:
      • Maxillary anterior: stop 0.5–1mm short of incisal edge
      • Maxillary posterior: stop ~1mm short of facial cusp tips/ridges
      • Mandibular anterior: stop 2mm short of incisal edges
      • Mandibular posterior: stop 2–2.5mm short of facial cusp tips/ridges
      • Teeth with wear facets: stop ~1.5mm short of the wear facet
  5. Cure all reservoirs with the curing light. Wear protective eyewear.
  6. After curing: Wipe the oxygen inhibition layer off the surface with a dry cloth or paper towel (wipe toward the incisal/occlusal surfaces, not toward the gingival margin).
  7. CRITICAL — Alcohol wipe: Thoroughly wipe all reservoir surfaces with a 2×2 gauze soaked in 99% isopropyl alcohol. Allow to dry completely. The isopropyl alcohol dissolves the smooth surface resin and exposes the rough filler particles underneath, creating a microscopically porous surface that allows air to move freely during thermoforming. After drying, the reservoir surface should appear dull — not shiny. If it still looks shiny, wipe again.
  8. Confirm reservoirs do not interfere with occlusion by checking against the opposing model.

Completed reservoir placement: blue KöR Light-Cure Block-Out Resin on all facial surfaces, first molar to first molar. Note reservoirs stop 1–1.5 mm from gingival margins and do not extend onto second molars.

Step 3 — Block Out the Model

Using the same KöR Light-Cure Block-Out Resin, lightly block out any of the following areas that could cause tray "fins" or make the tray difficult to insert:

  • Deep interproximal crevices or embrasures — very slightly fill with block-out resin and cure. Deep crevices form sharp fins in the tray that make insertion difficult.
  • Chips or rough/broken incisal edges — thin coat of block-out resin and cure.
  • Lingual retainer wires — lightly coat the areas immediately adjacent to retainer wires and cure. The resin coating should be virtually paper-thin.
  • Bubbles or voids in the printed model — fill with block-out resin and cure.

Step 4 — Apply Silicone/Teflon Separator Spray

🚫 Do NOT: Wet the model with water prior to thermoforming. Do NOT use Pam or any oil-based cooking spray. These will contaminate the model surface, prevent proper thermoforming, and/or permanently distort the tray when removing it from the model.
  1. The model must be completely dry, through-and-through before spraying. Moisture inside the model inhibits airflow and prevents proper tray formation.
  2. Spray the model thoroughly with Handler SLICK-88 / SPL-88 Dry Silicone/Teflon Spray. The model should look "totally wet" on all surfaces (except the bottom base surface). Coat liberally.
  3. Allow the model to dry completely through-and-through before placing in the Ministar S. The silicone/teflon spray must be fully dry before thermoforming.

Step 5 — Thermoform with the Ministar S (Pressure Machine)

  1. Place the model on the flat platform of the Ministar S. Do not use the pellet/bead tray.
  2. Load a round KöR EVA sheet (0.040" / 1.0 mm, soft) into the Ministar S frame. No paper cut-out is needed — pressure machines do not require it.
  3. Heat the EVA sheet per the Ministar S manufacturer's instructions until it begins to sag and become pliable. The EVA must be soft but not overheated — it should sag approximately ½ inch (13 mm) or more below the frame before pressing.
  4. Rapidly lower the heated EVA sheet down over the model and engage the pressure cycle per Ministar S instructions.
  5. Maintain pressure until the EVA is fully formed over the model. Keep heat on during the initial seconds of pressing to allow the softened material to adapt precisely to the gingival margin and crevice areas.
  6. After the EVA has formed over the model: while still under pressure, use a damp paper towel to cool the surface of the EVA over the model.
  7. The EVA must be 100% cooled to room temperature before separating the tray from the model. Removing the tray while still warm will cause permanent distortion. Do NOT use ice water or a refrigerator to cool — use a room-temperature water bath or allow to air-cool.
  8. Once fully cooled, separate the EVA from the model. Separation should be easy because of the silicone/teflon spray. If you have to tug hard to remove it, stop — you may permanently distort the tray. Re-spray the model and make a new suckdown.
  9. After separation, inspect the inner surface of the tray for a very thin, glossy "shiny line" at the gingival crevice area. This shiny line is the KöR-Seal — it indicates the tray has formed precisely into the gingival margin.

The KöR-Seal "shiny line" at the gingival crevice (top). The same line outlined in red for clarity (bottom). This glossy zone is the critical 1–1.5 mm peripheral seal — it must run continuously around the entire arch. If absent or broken, rethermoform.

Step 6 — Trim the KöR-Seal Tray

🚫 Trimming cautions — these will ruin the seal:
• NEVER flame polish — heat distorts the tray
• Do NOT use a hot knife tray trimmer — heat destroys the seal
• Do NOT use serrated scissors — leaves a rough, uncomfortable edge
  1. Trim the FACIAL margin: Using sharp, non-serrated KöR Scissors, cut exactly at the outer edge of the shiny gingival line. Do not leave any EVA material on the gingival side of the shiny line. The scissors should touch but not cut inside the shiny line. This creates the KöR-Seal at the 1–1.5mm gingival zone.

    2. Scissors cutting along the outer edge of the shiny gingival line (top). Red outline marks the cut line for clarity (bottom). Blades must touch but not pass inside the shiny line — no EVA left on the gingival side.

  2. Trim the LINGUAL margin: Do NOT scallop the lingual — trim with a non-scalloped straight line touching the gingival margin at the center of each tooth. A non-scalloped lingual is stronger and more comfortable for the patient.
  3. Place the trimmed tray back on the model to confirm the fit. The tray should sit precisely at the gingival margin all the way around. Do not leave the tray on the model for more than 1–2 minutes — trays will spring outward slightly when placed back and permanent distortion occurs if left on for longer.
  4. Store the tray loosely in the KöR Tray Case. After placing the tray in the case, gently shake — the tray should "rattle around" loosely inside. Do NOT cram the tray into the case tightly, as this will distort it.

Quality Check Before Dispensing to Patient

  • ✅ Shiny gingival seal line visible on the inside of the tray — all the way around both arches.
  • ✅ Facial margin trimmed precisely at the outer edge of the shiny line — no EVA left on gingival side.
  • ✅ Lingual/palatal margin trimmed non-scalloped, touching gingival margin at tooth centers.
  • ✅ Tray seated on model without rocking or gaps.
  • ✅ Reservoirs visible as slight bumps on facial surfaces, first molar to first molar.
  • ✅ No fins or sharp interproximal projections.
  • ✅ No holes or tears in the EVA material.
  • ✅ Tray stored loosely in KöR Case — rattles freely.
🖨️
Formlabs Form 4B — 3D Printer Operation & Maintenance
SOP-EQ-003  ·  Lab Technician / Clinical Assistant
Equipment
▾
SOP ID
SOP-EQ-003
Equipment
Formlabs Form 4B (dental SLA resin printer)
Software
PreForm (slicing & print preparation)
Post-Cure Unit
Formlabs Form Cure (UV + heat, 60°C)
Wash Unit
Formlabs Form Wash (IPA rinse)
Primary Resin
Formlabs Dental Model Resin or Formlabs Model Resin V2
🧤 Always wear nitrile gloves when handling uncured resin. Uncured liquid resin is a skin sensitizer. Avoid skin contact with the liquid resin, resin tank, and freshly printed parts before washing. Do not dispose of liquid resin down the drain — cure waste resin before disposal per local regulations.

PreForm — Preparing the Print File

  1. Open PreForm on the connected computer. Import the STL or OBJ file from the iTero or Allied Star export.
  2. Orient the model: click One-Click Print or manually orient with labial/buccal surfaces facing up to ensure the gingival margin area prints with maximum detail.
  3. Set base thickness to approximately 6.4 mm (¼ inch) in the base settings. Set lingual/palatal base to within ~6.4 mm of the teeth.
  4. Generate supports — use default settings or light touch-point supports. Confirm no supports are placed on gingival margin areas or occlusal surfaces.
  5. Select the correct resin type (Dental Model Resin or Model Resin V2) and confirm the layer thickness (typically 0.1 mm for model resin).
  6. Send to printer. The Form 4B will confirm the resin cartridge matches the selected resin — if not, swap cartridge before printing.

Print Operation

  1. Confirm the resin tank is installed, seated, and has sufficient resin. The Form 4B auto-fills from the cartridge — do not manually pour resin.
  2. Confirm the build platform is clean and locked into place.
  3. Press Print on the touchscreen (or confirm from PreForm). Monitor the first few layers for any adhesion issues.
  4. When complete, the display will indicate the print is finished. Do not leave prints on the build platform for extended periods after completion — resin can cure from ambient light and become difficult to remove.

Post-Processing — Wash & Cure

  1. Remove from build platform: The Form 4B is equipped with the Quick-Release Build Platform spring system — no additional tools are needed. Simply press the release lever/button to flex the platform and the print will detach cleanly. Handle with gloves — the part is still uncured resin.
  2. Form Wash — IPA rinse: Place the print in the Form Wash basket. Run for 10 minutes in fresh IPA (isopropyl alcohol). If IPA appears heavily discolored (dark orange), replace before washing new parts.
  3. Remove supports: After washing and air-drying (~5 minutes), remove support structures carefully with flush cutters or support removal tool. Do not apply leverage at the gingival margin area.
  4. Form Cure — UV cure: Place the model on the Form Cure turntable. Set temperature to 60°C and cure time to 10 minutes. Proper curing is required before using the model for tray fabrication — undercured resin will remain tacky and may not hold reservoirs properly.
  5. Allow the model to cool to room temperature before proceeding to tray fabrication (see SOP-KOR-007).

Routine Maintenance

FrequencyTask
After every printInspect the resin tank window for any cured resin debris or "failed layers." Remove with the spatula tool at a shallow angle — do not scratch the FEP film. Wipe build platform with IPA before reinstalling.
WeeklyCheck IPA level and clarity in Form Wash. Replace if heavily discolored. Wipe exterior of printer with a lint-free cloth.
MonthlyInspect FEP film in the resin tank for cloudiness, scratches, or cloudy patches — replace tank if FEP film is degraded (reduces print quality). Lubricate the build platform carriage per Formlabs instructions if prompted.
As neededReplace resin cartridge when empty. Do not mix resin types in the same tank. Replace tank if a print failure occurred due to cured resin on the FEP.
💡 Resin shelf life: Opened resin cartridges should be used within 12 months. Store resin cartridges in a cool, dark location — not in direct sunlight. Shake cartridge gently before each use to ensure pigment is evenly distributed.
🔥
MiniStar S — Pressure Thermoformer Operation & Maintenance
SOP-EQ-004  ·  Lab Technician / Clinical Assistant
Equipment
▾
SOP ID
SOP-EQ-004
Equipment
MiniStar S Pressure Thermoformer (Great Lakes / ScheuDental)
Type
Pressure-forming machine — superior adaptation vs. vacuum-only
Common Materials
KöR EVA (whitening trays) · Zendura A (aligners / retainers) · Essix ACE (retainers) · 3mm polypropylene (mouthguards)
⚠️ Always use the FLAT platform, not the pellet/bead tray. The bead tray is intended for thicker mouthguard material only and will produce inferior results for whitening trays or aligners. Use round sheets for KöR EVA; square sheets for Zendura/Essix.

General Operation

  1. Ensure the model is completely dry before thermoforming. Moisture prevents proper adaptation and will trap steam under the material.
  2. Place the model on the flat platform, centered. Ensure the model is properly prepared for the material being used (e.g., silicone/Teflon separator for KöR trays — see SOP-KOR-007).
  3. Load one sheet of the correct material into the frame, centered. Tighten the frame clamp finger-tight. Once packaging is opened, material must be used within 15 minutes.
  4. Select the appropriate program for the material being used (see material reference table below). The MiniStar S has preset programs — confirm the correct program is selected on the display before starting.
  5. Press Start. The heating element will warm the material. The machine will automatically apply pressure when the material reaches the correct temperature.
  6. Allow the full cycle to complete. Do not open the chamber during heating or pressurization.
  7. After the cycle ends, allow the model and material to cool adequately before separating. For KöR trays: cool to room temperature (use damp towel to accelerate). For aligners: allow 30–60 seconds before removing.
  8. Separate the thermoformed sheet from the model gently. If it does not release easily, do not force — allow additional cooling.

Material & Program Reference

MaterialUseSize / ShapeMiniSTAR S CodeMiniSTAR S Heat Time
Zendura AClear aligners & retainers0.030" (0.75 mm) · square132—
1 sheet per cycle · use within 15 min of opening · see SOP-CL-012
Zendura FLXClear aligners (flexible)0.030" (0.75 mm) · square132Same as Zendura A
1 sheet per cycle · use within 15 min of opening
Zendura VIVAClear aligners0.035" (0.89 mm) · square132Same as Zendura A
1 sheet per cycle · use within 15 min of opening
KöR EVAKöR whitening trays0.040" (1.0 mm) · round—Per SOP-KOR-007
Silicone spray model first · flat platform only
021-030 Mouthguard (Great Lakes)Mouthguards1 mm / 125 mm · round10230 sec
Patterned Clear Retainer (Great Lakes)Patterned/colored retainers0.040" / 125 mm · round16350 sec
Bioplast Xtreme Red (Scheu · REF 030-090)Sports mouthguards (reinforced)4 mm / 125 mm · round18360 sec
Clear Splint Biocryl (Great Lakes · REF 021-027)Splints2 mm / 125 mm · round—70 sec

Routine Maintenance

FrequencyTask
After each useRemove any debris or trimmed material from the pressure chamber and platform. Wipe the platform surface with a dry cloth.
WeeklyInspect the heating element for discoloration or damage. Check the pressure seal around the chamber for cracks or wear. Wipe the exterior with a damp cloth (unplugged).
As neededIf the machine fails to reach temperature or pressure, contact manufacturer. Do not attempt internal repairs — trained technician only.
😁
Scheu Biostar 4-Aligner — Positive Pressure Thermoformer
SOP-EQ-005  ·  Lab Technician / Clinical Assistant
Equipment
▾
SOP ID
SOP-EQ-005
Equipment
Scheu Biostar 4-Aligner (positive pressure thermoformer)
Technology
4 independent positive-pressure chambers · 4 bar (58 PSI) each
Capacity
Up to 4 aligners simultaneously · ~120–160 aligners/hour
Heating
Thermostatic infrared heaters (1 per chamber) — heats in 1–3 seconds
Materials Used
Zendura A · Zendura FLX · Zendura VIVA · Zendura AT (template) · Great Lakes Whitening Material
💡 Key advantage: The Biostar 4-Aligner uses positive pressure (not vacuum) to form the foil over the model at 4 bar. This produces superior adaptation to gingival margins and tooth surfaces compared to vacuum-forming machines. Each of the 4 chambers operates independently — you can run 1, 2, 3, or all 4 simultaneously.

Setup — Before First Use Each Session

  1. Confirm the air supply line (6 mm internal diameter) is connected and the supply pressure is ≥ 6 bar upstream (the machine regulates to 4 bar internally).
  2. Power on the unit. The color touchscreen will initialize and display the home screen.
  3. Confirm all 4 pressure chambers are clear of debris and that the O-ring at the bottom of each chamber is properly seated. A missing or displaced O-ring will prevent pressure from building.
  4. Confirm models (3D printed from iTero / Allied Star scan) are fully post-cured, dry, and at room temperature. Place each model on the corresponding platform tray.

Loading & Parameter Entry

  1. Remove the foil tray from the chamber by pulling the handles toward you.
  2. Place the foil strip or round foil onto the foil tray. Seat it flush on the support rails.
  3. Set parameters manually on the touchscreen per the material being used (see Material Reference table below). Enter the heating time and cooling time for the selected material.

Thermoforming Cycle

  1. Insert the foil tray: Slide the loaded foil tray fully to the back of the chamber until it clicks into place. Heating begins automatically as soon as the tray is fully inserted — do not interrupt.
  2. Heating phase: The infrared heater activates. Acoustic signals track progression — a series of beeps counts down the final seconds. Do not open the chamber during this phase.
  3. Pressure forming: When heating is complete, the machine automatically pressurizes the chamber to 4 bar, pressing the softened foil down over the model. The safety valve activates if pressure exceeds 5 bar.
  4. Cooling phase: The machine runs an automatic cooling cycle per the preset timing. Do not remove the tray early — premature removal causes distortion of the aligner.
  5. Completion: Acoustic signal indicates the cycle is complete. The touchscreen will indicate the chamber is ready for unloading.
  6. Remove the tray and separate the thermoformed appliance from the model. The appliance should release cleanly with slight flex. If it is stuck, allow 10–15 additional seconds of cooling before reattempting.

Material & Parameter Reference

MaterialApplicationHeating TimeCooling Time
Zendura AClear aligners50 sec60 sec
Zendura FLXClear aligners (flexible)50 sec60 sec
Zendura VIVAClear aligners50 sec60 sec
Zendura ATTemplate / overlay material40 sec60 sec
Great Lakes Whitening MaterialKöR whitening trays40 sec60 sec
⚠️ Always confirm parameters before starting. Enter the correct heating and cooling times on the touchscreen for the material selected. Using wrong parameters will produce a poorly adapted or distorted appliance.
⚠️ After forming: Send the thermoformed appliance to the OrthoAutomation Trimlign (SOP-EQ-006) for precision trimming before patient delivery. Inspect all edges — no sharp points, rough edges, or uneven scallops. Polish any rough edges before the patient tries it in.

Routine Maintenance

FrequencyTask
After each useRemove any material scraps or debris from the model platforms and foil tray rails. Wipe platforms clean with a dry cloth or air nozzle.
WeeklyInspect O-ring at the bottom of each pressure chamber — confirm it is seated and undamaged. Check the air inlet filter element for debris or moisture; replace if contaminated. Wipe exterior housing with a non-aggressive cleaner.
As neededUpdate firmware via the touchscreen interface when Scheu-Dental releases software updates. Contact Scheu-Dental or an authorized service technician for any internal repairs — do not attempt internal servicing.
🔧 The Biostar 4-Aligner infrared heating system is designed to be maintenance-free. The most common operational issue is pressure loss — always check O-ring seating first if a chamber fails to pressurize.
✂️
OrthoAutomation Trimlign — Aligner & Retainer Trimmer
SOP-EQ-006  ·  Lab Technician / Clinical Assistant
Equipment
▾
SOP ID
SOP-EQ-006
Equipment
OrthoAutomation Trimlign — automated aligner & retainer trimming machine
Application
Precision trimming of thermoformed aligners, retainers, and other thermoplastic appliances
Materials
Zendura A aligners · Essix ACE retainers · KöR whitening trays · any thermoformed appliance
▶ Background — Device Overview

Overview

The OrthoAutomation Trimlign is a dedicated trimming machine designed to cut thermoformed aligners and retainers precisely along the gingival margin. It produces a smooth, consistent trim line faster and more accurately than manual scissors for high-volume trimming. The machine uses a rotating blade or cutting mechanism guided along the appliance margin.

Setup

  1. Confirm the trimming blade/cutter is clean and sharp. A dull blade will produce a ragged edge — replace or sharpen per manufacturer guidelines before use if edge quality is poor.
  2. Select the correct trim setting for the appliance type (scalloped for aligners; straight or scalloped for retainers — per the case prescription).
  3. Power on the unit and allow it to reach operating speed before feeding any appliances.

Trimming Procedure

  1. Place the thermoformed appliance (still on or just removed from the model) into the Trimlign per the machine's loading instructions.
  2. Guide the appliance through the cutting path. The Trimlign follows the gingival margin contour to produce the trim line automatically.
  3. Trim settings by appliance type:
    ApplianceTrim StyleMargin Target
    Clear alignerScalloped0.5–1 mm above gingival margin per tooth
    Essix retainerScalloped or straight — per Dr. Akhavan preferenceAt or just above gingival margin
    KöR whitening trayFacial: outer edge of shiny line · Lingual: non-scalloped straightSee SOP-KOR-007 for full trimming detail
    Mouthguard / sportStraight — per prescription2–3 mm past gingival margin for retention
  4. After trimming, inspect all edges under good lighting. The trim line should be smooth with no sharp points, jagged edges, or uneven scallops.
  5. Smooth any rough edges with a polishing wheel, fine sandpaper strip, or by briefly passing the edge under the polishing attachment (if equipped). All edges must be comfortable to the touch before patient delivery.
  6. Seat the trimmed appliance on the model to verify the trim line is correct and consistent around the entire arch before delivery to the patient.
⚠️ Quality check before patient delivery: Run your fingertip around the entire gingival margin of the trimmed appliance. Any sharp edge or roughness must be polished smooth. Sharp edges cause gum irritation and patient complaints.

Routine Maintenance

FrequencyTask
After each useRemove all trimmed material scraps from the cutting area and collection tray. Wipe the cutting path and blade area with a dry cloth or soft brush.
WeeklyInspect the cutting blade for dullness, nicks, or debris buildup. Clean the blade carefully per manufacturer instructions. Check that all moving parts operate smoothly.
MonthlyLubricate moving parts per OrthoAutomation's maintenance schedule. Inspect electrical cord and housing for any damage.
As neededReplace cutting blade when trim quality degrades (ragged edges, inconsistent cut depth). Contact OrthoAutomation for replacement blade specifications.
💨
Vaniman Sandstorm Professional — Sandblaster Operation & Maintenance
SOP-EQ-007  ·  Clinical Assistant / Lab Technician
Equipment
▾
SOP ID
SOP-EQ-007
Equipment
Vaniman Sandstorm Professional Dental Sandblaster
Abrasive Media
50-micron aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) — standard dental grade
Air Supply
Compressed air — typically 60–80 PSI (4–5.5 bar)
Common Uses
Surface prep for bonding · cleaning metal brackets · roughening ceramic/zirconia for cementation · TAD site prep
🧤 PPE required during operation: Operator must wear eye protection (safety glasses or face shield) and a mask. Patient must have eyes protected — do not use intraorally without adequate isolation and patient protection. Aluminum oxide dust is an inhalation hazard.

Setup & Operation

  1. Confirm the aluminum oxide powder reservoir is filled with the correct abrasive media (50-micron Al₂O₃ for standard dental use). Do not mix different abrasive sizes in the reservoir.
  2. Connect to the compressed air supply. Set air pressure per the intended use (50–60 PSI for delicate surface prep; up to 80 PSI for heavy cleaning). Use the pressure regulator on the unit.
  3. Point the nozzle into the waste chamber and test the spray briefly before directing at the workpiece — confirms airflow and powder delivery are working correctly.
  4. Intraoral use (TAD site or bracket surface prep): Isolate the area with cotton rolls or rubber dam. Position suction immediately adjacent. Work in short 1–2 second bursts at 3–5 mm from the surface. Avoid directing spray toward soft tissue, eyes, or the throat.
  5. Lab use (bracket/band cleaning, appliance prep): Hold the workpiece in the gloved hand inside the sandblaster cabinet. Use the cabinet viewing window and glove ports. Direct spray at the target surface for 3–5 seconds per area.
  6. After use, release air pressure and cap the nozzle tip when not in immediate use to prevent powder from spilling.

Routine Maintenance

FrequencyTask
After each useWipe down the exterior. If equipped with a collection chamber/filter, check for accumulated waste powder and empty if needed.
WeeklyCheck the powder reservoir level and refill as needed. Inspect the nozzle tip for blockage or wear — replace nozzle tip if flow is inconsistent. Check the air filter/moisture trap and drain any accumulated water.
MonthlyInspect all air line connections for leaks. Clean the interior of the cabinet (if applicable) with a dry brush. Replace the HEPA filter cartridge per Vaniman's recommended schedule.
As neededReplace nozzle tip if worn or blocked. Replace powder if contaminated (discolored or clumped). Contact Vaniman for service if pressure regulation fails.
💡 Powder management: Keep the aluminum oxide powder in its sealed container when not in the reservoir — moisture causes clumping and can block the nozzle. If the powder clumps, it must be replaced; do not dry and reuse clumped abrasive.
📋
Appliance Tracking & Check-In Protocol
SOP-LB-001  ·  Sarah (Lab Coordinator)
Critical
▾
SOP ID
SOP-LB-001
Owner
Sarah
Tools
Tally (submission) · ASANA (task tracking)

Appliance Submission Workflow

  1. Patient commits to treatment and payment is collected.
  2. The clinical team member who scans or impresses for the appliance submits the order via the Tally appliance submission form (see SOP-CL-006 for the Tally link).
  3. Sarah reviews the submission, enters the appliance into the appropriate lab, and creates a tracking task in ASANA if follow-up is required.

Appliance Arrival & Check-In

  1. When the appliance arrives, Sarah opens and inspects the package.
  2. Sarah logs the arrival and marks the item as received in the tracking system.
  3. The appliance is placed in the appropriate designated location for the clinical team.
  4. Once the appliance is delivered chairside, the clinical team member who completed the appointment confirms delivery in the system.
🚨 Cross-check the appliance arrival log against due-date appointments every day. If an appliance has not arrived by its expected date, Sarah contacts the lab immediately and notifies the front desk to reschedule the patient if needed.

Tips

  • At the end of each day, every clinician should verify that all patients who were scanned that day have been submitted via Tally.
  • When checking in an appliance, confirm the patient's next appointment is correctly scheduled for the delivery visit.

No procedures found matching your search.

Try different keywords or clear the filter.

🦷 Orthodontic Clinical Manual  ·  Internal use only  ·  Always verify against current state regulations and practice policy
